audits.methodology: W3C Design Tokens + Atomic Design (Frost) + DRY

findings.title: Petrol blue #005863 is a real brand color used 5x but absent from the token kit
findings.evidence:
home.html:167 `
` and home.html:176 `style="color: #005863; font-weight: bold;"`; challenges.html:61 `box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 2px #005863;`; challenges.html:109 `.challenge-thumb-text i { ... color: #005863; }`; sql.html:8 `

Coming Soon

`. The comment on home.html:166 even names it: ``. The kit (style.css:8) only defines `--color-primary-blue: #00719a` — a visibly different teal. #005863 is a deliberate, repeated accent with no token.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: W3C Design Tokens: a recurring brand value MUST be a named token (single source of truth). DRY: the same hex is duplicated across 3 files/5 sites; one edit today requires 5 coordinated edits.
findings.recommendation: Add `--color-accent-petrol: #005863;` to the `:root` block in style.css (Colors – Secondary/Neutral group). Replace all 5 literals with `var(--color-accent-petrol)`. For the CTA button text-on-petrol (home.html:176), this is the same token. Consider also a `--color-accent-petrol-contrast: var(--color-primary-white)` if you want the on-petrol foreground tokenized.

findings.title: 'Inter' font loaded in base.html but never referenced — dead brand asset
findings.evidence: base.html:16 loads `...&family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700...` and the comment base.html:12 says `<!-- Google Fonts - Inter, Crimson Text, Libre Franklin -->`. A grep for `Inter` across all *.html/*.css finds it ONLY in this <link> and its comment — no `font-family` declaration in style.css or any template uses Inter. The kit's sans token (style.css:24) is `--font-family-sans: "Libre Franklin", ...` (not Inter).
findings.methodologyPrinciple: DRY / single source of truth: the loaded typeface set must equal the tokenized typeface set. Loading a font the token layer never names is dead weight and a false 'brand kit' signal (Atomic Design: atoms declared but never composed).
findings.recommendation: Remove `Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&` from the Google Fonts URL (base.html:16) and update the comment on base.html:12 to drop 'Inter'. This is one canonical font-loading line; no token change needed since no token references Inter.

findings.title: 'JetBrains Mono' is used for code/monospace but has no token in the kit
findings.evidence: base.html:16 loads `JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;500`. It is then hardcoded as a raw literal 3x: admin.html:158 `font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace !important;`, challenge_detail.html:353 `.CodeMirror { ... font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; }`, challenge_detail.html:361 `.test-item code { ... font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; }`. The Typography token group (style.css:22-24) defines only serif and sans — no `--font-family-mono`, despite mono being a first-class part of a coding portfolio.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: W3C Design Tokens: the mono typeface is a design primitive (an 'atom') and must be a named token, not a literal repeated at 3 call sites. DRY: 3 duplicate stacks; changing the code font requires editing 3 places in 2 files.
findings.recommendation: Add `--font-family-mono: "JetBrains Mono", "SFMono-Regular", Consolas, monospace;` to the Typography block in style.css (after --font-family-sans). Replace the 3 literals with `var(--font-family-mono)`. Keep `!important` only where currently needed (admin.html:158).

findings.title: Button hover blue #005a7d hardcoded in the token file itself instead of being a token
findings.evidence: style.css:144-148 `.btn-primary:hover { background-color: #005a7d !important; /* Darker blue */ border-color: #005a7d !important; }`. This 'darker primary blue' is a derived brand shade living as a raw literal inside the canonical stylesheet — the one file that should contain zero un-tokenized hexes.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: W3C Design Tokens: derived/state colors (hover, active) should be tokens (e.g. a -dark variant), so the palette is fully described in :root. DRY: the value is duplicated on the same rule (bg + border).
findings.recommendation: Add `--color-primary-blue-dark: #005a7d;` to the Colors – Primary group and reference it in the two `.btn-primary:hover` declarations. This makes the interaction palette part of the single token layer.

findings.title: Ungoverned grayscale/UI palette (#f8f9fa, #343a40, #f1f3f5, #212529, #e9ecef, #adb5bd, #000, #333, #666, #ccc, #cccccc, #eee...) hardcoded across every template
findings.evidence: The neutral surface #f8f9fa alone appears ~10x (admin.html:131, challenges.html:32, challenge_detail.html:217/286/294, home.html:64/199, register.html:5/18, dashboard.html:5, verify_otp.html:5). The dark neutral #343a40 appears 6x in challenges.html (15,24,26,33,75,102). Code-block dark #212529 in home.html:136 and challenge_detail.html:293. Chip gray #f1f3f5 in challenges.html:59/103/275, challenge_detail.html:358, home.html:192. Plus admin.html's own micro-palette: #000, #333, #ccc, #666, #fafafa, #f0f0f0, #eee, #b4b4b4 (lines 142-191). The kit only ships 3 neutrals: --color-neutral-dark #333333, -medium #666666, -light #ebebeb (style.css:18-20). None of the Bootstrap-derived grays above map to those, so the 'single canonical token layer' is bypassed on every page.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: W3C Design Tokens single-source-of-truth + Atomic Design: neutrals are atoms and must be a defined scale consumed by all templates. DRY: the same handful of grays are re-typed dozens of times; there is no authoritative representation, so the palette drifts (e.g. #333333 token vs #333 literal vs Bootstrap $gray-800).
findings.recommendation: Extend the token kit with a neutral scale that names the values already in use, then replace literals with vars. Concretely add: `--color-surface-subtle: #f8f9fa;` (light section bg), `--color-surface-muted: #f1f3f5;` (chips/code inline), `--color-surface-disabled: #e9ecef;` (empty calendar cell), `--color-neutral-800: #343a40;` (calendar borders/headers), `--color-neutral-900: #212529;` (code block bg), `--color-neutral-400: #adb5bd;` (muted day number). Map the admin.html one-offs onto existing tokens: #000→var(--color-primary-black), #333→var(--color-neutral-dark), #666→var(--color-neutral-medium), #ccc/#eee→a new `--color-border-subtle`. Because these values equal Bootstrap 5's gray scale, define them once in :root and delete the per-template literals.

findings.title: GitHub brand colors #24292e / #1a1e22 and neutral #ddd hardcoded inline in register.html
findings.evidence: register.html:22 `style="background-color: #24292e; color: #ffffff; ..."` and register.html:23 `onmouseover="this.style.backgroundColor='#1a1e22'" onmouseout="this.style.backgroundColor='#24292e'"`. The GitHub base #24292e is duplicated 3x (once in style, twice in inline JS). The Google button border #ddd (register.html:17) and hover #f8f9fa (register.html:18) are likewise raw.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: W3C Design Tokens: third-party brand colors are still tokens ('brand/social' category) — GitHub dark used in 3 places must resolve to one name. DRY: the hover/rest pair is triplicated across style + two JS event attributes.
findings.recommendation: Add `--color-brand-github: #24292e;` and `--color-brand-github-hover: #1a1e22;` to a 'Brand / social' token group. Move the hover behavior out of inline onmouseover/onmouseout into a `.btn-social-github` class in style.css using the tokens (`:hover { background: var(--color-brand-github-hover); }`), eliminating the JS color swap entirely (see BT-8). For the Google button, use `--color-border-subtle` (#ddd) and `--color-surface-subtle` (#f8f9fa) from BT-5.

findings.title: Accent literals #d63384 / #0dcaf0 / #a70e13 / #198754 duplicate or bypass existing tokens
findings.evidence: challenge_detail.html:289 `.markdown-content code { ... color: #d63384; }` (Bootstrap pink for inline code) vs challenge_detail.html:363 where `.test-item code` uses `var(--color-primary-red)` — two different colors for the same 'inline code' concept, an inconsistency. home.html:138 `color: #0dcaf0;` (Bootstrap cyan, 'Solution Preview' heading) is a brand accent with no token. challenges.html:395 `<span id="modalNumber" class="badge" style="background-color:#a70e13; ...">` hardcodes the exact value of the existing token `--color-primary-red: #a70e13` (style.css:9) instead of using it. admin.html:181-183 uses #198754 (Bootstrap success green) where the kit already defines `--color-secondary-green: #007a3d`.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: W3C Design Tokens / DRY: BT-7's #a70e13 is the textbook violation — a literal that is byte-for-byte an existing token, proving the token exists but isn't consumed. The green/pink/cyan cases show ad-hoc accents competing with defined secondary tokens (Atomic Design: atoms bypassed).
findings.recommendation: challenges.html:395 → replace `background-color:#a70e13` with `background-color: var(--color-primary-red)` (or better, add class `badge-challenge-number` using the token). challenge_detail.html:289 → reconcile inline-code color: use `var(--color-primary-red)` to match .test-item code, or introduce `--color-code-inline`. admin.html success states → map #198754 to `--color-secondary-green` (or add `--color-feedback-success` if you want to keep Bootstrap green distinct). home.html:138 cyan → add `--color-accent-cyan: #0dcaf0` if it is intentional brand, else recolor to a kit token.

findings.title: Heavy inline style="" blocks (and inline JS style swaps) that should be reusable classes
findings.evidence: Repeated inline 'badge' styling: home.html:41 and challenge_detail.html:22 both carry the identical string `style="background-color: var(--color-primary-red); letter-spacing: 1px; font-family: var(--font-family-sans);"` for the challenge-number badge. The 'card-header on dark' white text `style="letter-spacing: 1px; ...; color: #ffffff !important;"` recurs at challenge_detail.html:88,114,211. The emoji rating spans (challenge_detail.html:219-224) each repeat `style="cursor: pointer; transition: 0.2s;" onmouseover="this.style.transform='scale(1.2)'" onmouseout="this.style.transform='scale(1)'"` five times. Footer text style `style="color: #cccccc; font-size: 0.85rem;"` duplicated base.html:72 & 78 (and #cccccc already equals footer a color in style.css:215).
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Atomic Design: recurring visual patterns (badge, dark card-header, rating chip) are molecules and must be defined once as classes, not re-declared inline per instance. DRY: identical style strings are copy-pasted across templates; inline styles also cannot consume the cascade or be themed centrally.
findings.recommendation: Promote the repeated inline patterns to classes in style.css: (1) `.badge-challenge-number { background-color: var(--color-primary-red); letter-spacing:1px; font-family: var(--font-family-sans); }` — replace home.html:41 & challenge_detail.html:22. (2) `.card-header-dark h5, .on-dark-title { color: var(--color-primary-white); }` so the three `color:#ffffff !important` inline styles drop. (3) `.rating-emoji { cursor:pointer; transition: transform .2s; } .rating-emoji:hover { transform: scale(1.2); }` — replaces the 5 inline onmouseover/onmouseout handlers (challenge_detail.html:219-224). (4) Footer: reuse a `.footer-fineprint` class instead of inline `#cccccc`. This collapses dozens of inline strings into the single canonical token-driven layer.

findings.title: Per-page <style> blocks embedded in templates fragment the canonical CSS layer
findings.evidence: Three templates re-define `.markdown-content` styling in local <style> blocks with divergent values: home.html:191-197 `.markdown-content code { background:#f1f3f5; ... color: var(--color-primary-red); }` vs challenge_detail.html:285-290 `.markdown-content code { background:#f8f9fa; ... color:#d63384; }` — same selector, different background AND text color depending on page. challenges.html embeds ~277 lines of calendar/modal CSS inline. admin.html embeds its whole form theme (128-199). None of this lives in style.css, so 'the canonical token layer every page consumes' does not exist — each page carries its own mini-stylesheet.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: DRY / single source of truth: the same component (.markdown-content) has multiple authoritative definitions that disagree. Atomic Design: component CSS should live in one shared layer, not be redefined per template.
findings.recommendation: Extract the shared component blocks (.markdown-content, calendar grid, modal panels, paste-zone/kbd) into style.css (or partials like static/css/components/*.css) so there is one definition. Reconcile the two conflicting .markdown-content code rules into a single tokenized rule (`background: var(--color-surface-muted); color: var(--color-primary-red);`). Page-specific one-offs that genuinely can't be shared can stay, but token values inside them must reference vars, not literals.

findings.title: Spacing/radius tokens exist but are ignored in templates (raw px/rem everywhere)
findings.evidence: The kit defines a full spacing scale (--spacing-xs..xxl, style.css:27-32) and radii (--radius-sm 2px, --radius-md 4px, style.css:35-37). Yet templates use raw values that match the scale but don't reference it: admin.html:130 `padding: 50px 0;`, admin.html:171 `border-radius: 8px;`, challenges.html:79/226 `border-radius: 4px;` (== --radius-md), challenge_detail.html:294-297 `padding: 1rem; border-radius: 4px;`. #005863 inset uses `2px` (== --radius-sm value coincidentally). The radius token is essentially unused outside style.css's own .btn/.badge.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: W3C Design Tokens: spacing and radius are tokens on equal footing with color; hardcoding 4px/8px where --radius-md/--spacing-* exist defeats the token system. DRY: magic numbers duplicated instead of referencing the scale.
findings.recommendation: Replace radius literals that equal a token with the var (e.g. `border-radius: var(--radius-md)` for the 4px cases in challenges.html:79,226 and challenge_detail.html). For 8px/12px (admin card/paste-zone) either snap to the existing scale or add `--radius-lg: 8px;`/`--radius-xl: 12px;`. Convert obvious spacing literals (50px→closest --spacing-* or add --spacing-section) as a follow-up. Lower priority than color, but required for a truly 'single canonical token layer'.

topPriorities: BT-1: Tokenize petrol blue #005863 — it is a deliberate, repeated brand accent (CTA, calendar hover, SQL hero) with zero token; add --color-accent-petrol and replace all 5 literals. Highest brand-integrity payoff for lowest risk.

BT-3 + BT-2: Fix the typography layer — add --font-family-mono for JetBrains Mono (used 3x, no token) and remove the dead 'Inter' font from base.html. This makes the loaded fonts equal the tokenized fonts.

BT-5: Establish a neutral/surface token scale (#f8f9fa, #343a40, #f1f3f5, #212529, #e9ecef, #adb5bd, admin grays). This is the single largest source of un-tokenized hexes and the main reason no canonical layer currently exists.
BT-7 (#a70e13) : Immediately replace the challenges.html:395 literal with var(--color-primary-red) — it is byte-identical to an existing token, the clearest DRY violation.

BT-8 + BT-9: Promote repeated inline style strings and per-page <style> blocks (esp. the conflicting .markdown-content and duplicated badge/rating/dark-header patterns) into shared token-driven classes so every page consumes one layer.
audits.summary: The repo ships a genuine W3C-style token kit in static/css/style.css:6-38 (colors, two font families, a full spacing scale, two radii), but that kit is NOT the single source of truth the brief asks for — templates bypass it pervasively. Findings fall into four buckets. (1) Missing tokens for real brand values: petrol blue #005863 (5 uses across home/challenges/sql, explicitly labeled 'Petrol Blue' in a comment) and JetBrains Mono (3 uses) are first-class brand primitives with no token; the button-hover shade #005a7d is even hardcoded inside the token file itself. (2) A dead asset: the 'Inter' Google Font is loaded in base.html:16 but referenced nowhere. (3) An entirely ungoverned neutral/UI palette — the Bootstrap-derived grays #f8f9fa (~10x), #343a40 (6x), #f1f3f5, #212529, #e9ecef, #adb5bd plus admin.html's private micro-palette (#000/#333/#666/#ccc/#eee/#b4b4b4) — none of which map to the kit's three neutrals, so every page re-types its own grays. (4) Token bypass where a token already exists: challenges.html:395 hardcodes #a70e13, the exact value of --color-primary-red; two .markdown-content code rules disagree on color; admin uses #198754 where --color-secondary-green exists; spacing/radius tokens are ignored in favor of raw 4px/8px/50px. Structurally, three templates embed large per-page <style> blocks and dozens of duplicated inline style='' strings (identical badge, dark card-header, and 5x emoji-rating patterns), so there is currently no canonical layer every page consumes. Recommended path: extend :root with an accent-petrol token, a --font-family-mono token, a named neutral/surface scale, and social-brand tokens; delete the Inter font; replace all literals with vars; and promote the recurring inline patterns and conflicting per-page component CSS into shared, token-driven classes. All fixes are client-side (templates + one CSS file), none are server-only; the color/typography changes are low-risk find-and-replace, while the neutral-scale consolidation and inline-to-class refactors (BT-5/8/9) are larger and carry moderate visual-regression risk that should be verified against each rendered page.

audits.methodology: WCAG 2.2 Level AA (W3C Recommendation) cross-referenced with Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics. Every finding cites the specific Success Criterion (SC) and/or heuristic it violates. Contrast ratios were computed with the WCAG relative-luminance formula (see verification run).

findings.title: Global keydown handler blocks F12/Ctrl+Shift+I/J/Ctrl+U — hostile to assistive tech, useless as security
findings.evidence: document.onkeydown = function (e) { if (e.keyCode == 123) { return false; } ... } and document.addEventListener('contextmenu', function (e) { e.preventDefault(); });
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (A) — all functionality must be operable via keyboard without the interface intercepting/cancelling keys; SC 2.1.4 Character Key Shortcuts (A) — single-key shortcuts must be avoidable. Assigning to document.onkeydown also clobbers any other keydown listener. The contextmenu block removes the platform right-click menu that screen-reader/AT and translation tools rely on. Nielsen H7 (Flexibility & efficiency) and H10 (Help): it strips standard OS affordances. Per KG 08-security S-12 this is cosmetic-only and trivially bypassed, so the a11y cost buys nothing.
findings.recommendation: Delete both blocks (base.html lines 91-112 <script>). If a deterrent is truly desired, it belongs server-side, not in keyboard interception. Removing them restores keyboard/AT operability at zero security loss.

findings.title: pointer-events:none on modal image plus oncontextmenu block prevents legitimate image interaction
findings.evidence: #modalImage { ... pointer-events: none; /* Stops drag and drop */ } and
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A) — users must be able to reach/inspect the image (e.g. 'open image in new tab' to zoom with browser AT); pointer-events:none also blocks pointer users from any per-image action. Nielsen H4 (Consistency & standards): disabling the OS context menu breaks the expected image affordance. Same cosmetic-only anti-copy rationale as A11Y-01.
findings.recommendation: Remove pointer-events:none and the oncontextmenu handler from #modalImage. The Ctrl+Scroll zoom is handled on the wrapper via a wheel listener, so it does not depend on pointer-events being disabled.

findings.title: Calendar day tiles are non-focusable, non-keyboard-operable div click targets
findings.evidence: <div class="calendar-day ... has-challenge" ... onclick="handleDateClick('{{ date_str }}')"> with cursor:pointer set in CSS but no tabindex, no role, no keydown handler; there is a #challenge-link CSS class defined but the challenge is rendered as a bare div, not an <a>.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (A) — a div with onclick is not reachable or activatable by keyboard; SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — a clickable div exposes no button/link role to screen readers. Nielsen H6 (Recognition): keyboard/SR users cannot recognize or reach the day as interactive.
findings.recommendation: Render each has-challenge day as a real <a href="/challenge/{{challenge.date_id}}"> (progressive enhancement: JS can still preventDefault to open the modal) OR add role="button" tabindex="0" plus a keydown handler firing handleDateClick on Enter/Space, and an aria-label like 'Open challenge for {{date_str}}: {{challenge.title}}'. An <a> is preferred because it also gives a working no-JS fallback.

findings.title: Emoji satisfaction ratings are click-only <span>s — not keyboard/SR operable, no accessible name
findings.evidence: 🤩 (repeated for 🙂 😐 🙁 😭)
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (A) — span onclick is not keyboard-focusable/activatable; SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — raw emoji glyph gives an SR an unreliable/absent accessible name and no button role; SC 1.4.1 Use of Color/graphics — the emotion is conveyed only by the emoji image. Nielsen H1 (Visibility of system status) is fine but access is broken.
findings.recommendation: Convert each rating to <button type="button" class="btn btn-link p-0" aria-label="Rate: Very satisfied" onclick="submitRating('🤩')">🤩</button>. Group them in a container with role="group" aria-label="Rate this solution". Buttons are natively focusable and expose a role; aria-label supplies the semantic meaning of each emoji.

findings.title: Bootstrap navbar-toggler has no aria-controls / aria-expanded / aria-label
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — the toggle button has no accessible name (icon is a pure CSS background span) and does not announce collapsed/expanded state; SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A). This is the standard Bootstrap pattern that was left incomplete (docs require aria-controls/aria-expanded/aria-label).
findings.recommendation: Add aria-controls="navbarNav" aria-expanded="false" aria-label="Toggle navigation" to the button. Bootstrap's collapse JS will flip aria-expanded automatically once aria-expanded is present.

findings.title: Empty calendar day number #adb5bd on white fails AA text contrast (2.07:1)
findings.evidence: .day-number { ... color: #adb5bd; ... } on .calendar-day { background-color: white; } — computed contrast 2.07:1 (has-challenge days override to #343a40, but days without a challenge keep #adb5bd).
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) (AA) — normal-size text requires >= 4.5:1; 2.07:1 is well below. The day number is meaningful information (which calendar date).
findings.recommendation: Darken the non-challenge day-number color to at least #6c757d (4.69:1 on white) or #767676. Keep the has-challenge override as-is (already 10.34:1).

findings.title: 'Let's Connect' body copy uses text-white-50 on #005863 — fails AA (2.94:1)
findings.evidence:
...

Interested in my journey..." — text-white-50 is rgba(255,255,255,.5); effective ~2.94:1 on #005863.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) (AA) — 1.1rem (~17.6px) non-bold body text needs 4.5:1; 2.94:1 fails. The section comment even claims 'Strict High-Contrast' which is inaccurate.
findings.recommendation: Replace text-white-50 with text-white (21:1) or a custom rgba(255,255,255,0.85) (>=4.5:1) for this paragraph. Verify the final swatch >= 4.5:1 against #005863.

findings.title: FCC description injected as raw HTML with `| safe` — headings/structure not normalized, potential nested landmark/heading issues and unescaped content
findings.evidence: {{ daily_challenge.fcc_description | safe }} rendered inside a <div class="challenge-description"> with no control over heading levels emitted by FCC's HTML.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A) / SC 2.4.6 Headings and Labels (AA) — externally-sourced HTML may emit heading levels that break the page's h1>h2>h3 outline (home.html's own <h2> is at line 97, FCC content sits under it). Nielsen H4 (Consistency).
findings.recommendation:
Sanitize/normalize FCC HTML server-side (e.g. bleach allowlist, and demote/strip heading tags to

/ or a fixed level) before storing/rendering, so the document outline stays coherent.

findings.title: Decorative Bootstrap icons lack aria-hidden; icon-only links/buttons rely on adjacent text but icons still announced
findings.evidence: Widespread used purely decoratively (e.g. View Solution) without aria-hidden="true"; the two social links in home.html:206-218 correctly use aria-label but their icons are not hidden.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A) — decorative icons should be hidden from AT so they are not announced as spurious content. The social <a> already has aria-label, so the icon is redundant to a screen reader.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-hidden="true" to all purely-decorative <i class="bi ..."> icons (they are always paired with visible text or an aria-label). Fast global pass across templates.

findings.title: Manual 'Register Manually' toggle hides the entire form by default with no aria-expanded, and social login <a> buttons lack accessible names
findings.evidence: controls #manualFields (class d-none) with no aria-controls/aria-expanded. Google contains only with no alt; GitHub contains only an icon — neither link has an accessible name.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — the toggle does not expose expanded/collapsed state; the Google/GitHub links have no accessible name (SC 2.4.4 Link Purpose (A), SC 1.1.1 for the img). Nielsen H1 (Visibility of system status): a keyboard/SR user cannot tell the manual form appeared.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-controls="manualFields" aria-expanded="false" to the toggle and flip aria-expanded in the onclick. Add alt="Sign in with Google" to the and aria-label="Sign in with GitHub" to the GitHub (plus aria-hidden on its ).

findings.title: Live client-side validation errors are visual-only (d-none toggle, no aria-live / aria-invalid / aria-describedby)
findings.evidence: <small id="emailFeedback" class="form-text text-danger d-none">Invalid Email Structure</small> shown by removing d-none; the input has no aria-invalid or aria-describedby, and submitBtn.disabled is toggled with no explanation announced.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.1 Error Identification (A) — errors must be programmatically associated with the field and announced; SC 4.1.3 Status Messages (AA) — dynamically revealed messages need a live region. A disabled submit button that never explains why also violates Nielsen H9 (Help users recognize/recover from errors).
findings.recommendation: Add aria-describedby="emailFeedback"/"mobileFeedback" to the inputs; set aria-invalid="true"/"false" in validate(); give each feedback <small> role="alert" (or wrap in aria-live="polite"). Prefer showing an inline error on submit over silently disabling the button, or add aria-describedby text explaining the disabled state.

findings.title: OTP input is type=text with no autocomplete=one-time-code, no inputmode, and no programmatic label association issue but no numeric hint
findings.evidence: <label for="otp">Enter 6-Digit PIN</label> <input type="text" id="otp" name="otp" ... maxlength="6" placeholder="000000"> (no inputmode/autocomplete)
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.3.5 Identify Input Purpose (AA) — a one-time-code field should declare its purpose; helps AT and mobile keypad users. Nielsen H7 (Efficiency of use): missing numeric keypad hint.
findings.recommendation: Add inputmode="numeric" autocomplete="one-time-code" pattern="[0-9]{6}" to the OTP input. Label/for association is already correct.

findings.title: Admin paste-zone divs are click/paste targets with no keyboard role and CSS removes focus outline
findings.evidence:
Click here & Press Ctrl+V to Paste Image
; JS does zone.addEventListener('click', () => zone.focus()) but a div is not focusable without tabindex; .paste-zone:focus { ... outline: none; } removes the focus ring.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (A) — the paste zone cannot be tab-reached (no tabindex); SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA) — outline:none removes the visible focus indicator; SC 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured is fine but Focus Visible is violated.
findings.recommendation: Add tabindex="0" and role="button" (or better, rely on the real <input type=file> which is already keyboard-accessible and make the paste-zone purely supplementary aria-hidden). Remove outline:none in .paste-zone:focus or replace with a visible :focus-visible box-shadow. Since a working file input already exists, the div is an enhancement and should not be the only path.

findings.title: Dropdown 'Daily Challenges' toggle uses href="#" losing focus target; images use title as alt (acceptable) but home img alt duplicates nearby heading
findings.evidence:
Daily Challenges; home image sits inside an whose destination and adjacent

both are the same title (redundant announcement).
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.4 Link Purpose (A) / SC 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A) — a linked image whose alt repeats the adjacent visible heading text causes double announcement; the href="#" dropdown toggle is OK because role=button + aria-expanded are present, but consider type=button. Nielsen H8 (Aesthetic/minimalist): reduce redundant SR output.
findings.recommendation: For the home thumbnail link, set alt="" (decorative, since the adjacent <h2> and link text already convey it) or make the alt describe it as 'Screenshot: {{title}}'. The dropdown is minor — acceptable as-is since aria-expanded/role are present.

findings.title: No skip-to-content link; single <main> exists but keyboard users must tab through nav on every page
findings.evidence:
{% block content %}{% endblock %}
with no preceding Skip to content; nav has no landmark label beyond implicit.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks (A) — a mechanism to skip repeated navigation must be provided. Nielsen H7 (Flexibility & efficiency of use).
findings.recommendation:
Add id="main" to
and insert as the first body child Skip to main content. Bootstrap ships .visually-hidden-focusable.

findings.title: Modal (lightbox) is not a real dialog: no role=dialog/aria-modal, no focus trap, no focus return, close is a click-only <span>
findings.evidence: ... × — no role="dialog", no aria-modal="true", no aria-labelledby; the × close is a (not focusable/keyboard-activatable); focus is never moved into the modal on open nor returned to the triggering day on close. (Escape-to-close is implemented, which is good.)
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — the dialog exposes no dialog role/name; SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (A) — the close control is a non-focusable span; SC 2.4.3 Focus Order (A) — focus is neither moved into the opened dialog nor restored to the trigger on close; SC 2.4.11 (2.2) focus handling. Nielsen H3 (User control & freedom): keyboard users cannot cleanly enter/exit.
findings.recommendation: Give #imageModal role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-labelledby="modalTitle"; make #modalClose a <button type="button" aria-label="Close">; on open, move focus to the close button and trap Tab within the modal; on close, return focus to the day element that opened it. Escape handling already exists — keep it.

findings.title: Test pass/fail status conveyed by color + icon only, injected into a non-live console; no programmatic status for SR users
findings.evidence: icon.className = 'bi bi-check-circle-fill ... text-success' vs 'bi bi-x-circle-fill ... text-danger'; results appended to <div id="consoleOutput"> which has no aria-live; 'ALL TESTS PASSED' is a visual innerHTML append.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.1 Use of Color (A) — pass/fail must not rely on green/red icon alone (though the icon shape helps, there is no text label per test); SC 4.1.3 Status Messages (AA) — the console updates are not announced. Nielsen H1 (Visibility of system status) for SR users.
findings.recommendation: Add role="log" aria-live="polite" to #consoleOutput. Set aria-label on each test-icon reflecting state ('Test passed'/'Test failed') or append visually-hidden text. Ensure the 'ALL TESTS PASSED' message lands in the live region so it is announced.

findings.title: CodeMirror editor initialized from a textarea with no associated label; interpreter region has no accessible name
findings.evidence: <textarea id="codeEditor"># Type your python code here...</textarea> replaced by CodeMirror.fromTextArea; no <label for="codeEditor"> and no aria-label on the generated CodeMirror widget.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: WCAG 2.2 SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A) / SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — the code entry field has no programmatic label. CodeMirror 5's contenteditable surface needs an explicit accessible name.
findings.recommendation: Add a visually-hidden <label for="codeEditor">Python code editor</label>, and after init set editor.getWrapperElement().setAttribute('aria-label','Python code editor'). Note: CodeMirror 5 has known SR limitations — at minimum name the region.

findings.title: Native alert() dialogs used for user flows (time-machine guard, code-copied, Get In Touch) — disruptive, unstyled, focus-stealing
findings.evidence: alert("Ah.. You will need a Time Machine for that..."), alert("[Fact: Daily coding challenge started from 11th August 2025]"), alert("Code copied to clipboard!"), onclick="alert('A bot coming vesr soon...')"
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Nielsen H1 (Visibility of system status) and H10 (Help) are met crudely, but native alert() cannot be styled, blocks the thread, and provides an inconsistent AT experience; a copy confirmation should be a non-modal status (SC 4.1.3 Status Messages, AA). H8 (Aesthetic/minimalist) and H4 (Consistency).
findings.recommendation: Replace alert() copy-confirmation with an inline aria-live status ('Code copied'). Replace the date-guard alerts with a message rendered inside the modal/inline region using role=status. Low priority but improves consistency and AT behavior.

topPriorities: A11Y-01: Delete the base.html contextmenu + keydown blockers (F12/Ctrl+Shift+I/J/Ctrl+U) — they violate SC 2.1.1/2.1.4, clobber document.onkeydown, and buy zero security (confirmed cosmetic in KG S-12). Trivial, safe, affects every page.

A11Y-03 & A11Y-04: Make the calendar day tiles and emoji rating spans real keyboard-operable controls (anchor/button + role + aria-label). Right now core interactions — opening any challenge and rating it — are completely unreachable by keyboard/screen-reader users (SC 2.1.1, 4.1.2).

A11Y-16: Turn the lightbox into a real dialog (role=dialog, aria-modal, focusable <button> close, focus move-in/return, focus trap). Keyboard users can open it but cannot properly operate or exit it via the visible close control.
A11Y-06 & A11Y-07: Fix the two AA contrast failures — calendar day number #adb5bd on white (2.07:1) and the 'Let's Connect' text-white-50 on #005863 (2.94:1).

A11Y-05 & A11Y-10 & A11Y-11: Complete ARIA on interactive widgets — navbar-toggler aria-label/aria-expanded, register social-login accessible names + toggle aria-expanded, and live-region/aria-invalid wiring for the register validation errors.
audits.summary: Audited all 9 templates and style.css against WCAG 2.2 AA + Nielsen heuristics, with contrast ratios computed numerically. The recently-fixed Ack-pattern modal fetch and calendar hover flicker were confirmed and excluded. The most serious issues are operability, not aesthetics: (1) base.html globally intercepts F12/Ctrl+Shift+I/J/Ctrl+U via document.onkeydown and blocks the context menu — hostile to AT and, per the knowledge graph's own S-12, cosmetic-only security; (2) the two primary interactions — opening a challenge from the calendar (div+onclick) and rating a solution (span+onclick emoji) — are keyboard- and screen-reader-inaccessible (SC 2.1.1, 4.1.2); (3) the lightbox is not a real dialog (no role/aria-modal, non-focusable span close, no focus management). Two genuine AA contrast failures were confirmed by calculation: empty-day calendar numbers (#adb5bd on white, 2.07:1) and the 'Let's Connect' body text (text-white-50 on #005863, 2.94:1); most other palette pairs pass. Additional gaps: missing skip link (SC 2.4.1), incomplete navbar-toggler ARIA, register social-login links with no accessible name and a toggle with no aria-expanded, validation errors that are visual-only with no aria-live/aria-invalid, unlabeled CodeMirror editor, test results conveyed by color/icon in a non-live console, admin paste-zones that are unfocusable divs with outline:none, and disruptive native alert() flows. Most fixes are trivial-to-small and safe (add ARIA attributes, swap div/span for a/button, darken two colors); A11Y-08 (sanitize FCC HTML for heading structure) is server-only and A11Y-16 (dialog focus trap) is medium effort. No finding requires the server to be running to remediate except the FCC-HTML normalization.

audits.methodology: Mobile-first responsive design + fluid layout (Ethan Marcotte, "Responsive Web Design": fluid grids, flexible images, media queries; progressive enhancement from small viewport up) + CSS Grid/Flexbox best practice (intrinsic/content-aware track sizing via minmax()/auto-fit, container queries for component-level responsiveness, fluid typography via clamp()).

findings.title: Calendar grid is hard-locked to 7 equal columns (repeat(7, 1fr)) with no small-viewport reflow — cells collapse to ~40px on phones
findings.evidence: .calendar-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(7, 1fr); ... } and the only responsive rule is @media (max-width: 768px){ .calendar-day{ min-height: 80px } } — the column count never changes. On a 320px viewport minus container padding (~288px) each of 7 columns is ~41px wide, so .challenge-thumb-text span with -webkit-line-clamp:3 at font-size:0.72rem and the absolutely-positioned .day-number both crowd the same ~41px box.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte fluid grid + CSS Grid best practice: grid tracks should be content-aware (minmax/auto-fit) so the layout adapts to available space rather than forcing a fixed column count. A month calendar is semantically 7 columns, but at phone widths the fixed 7-up makes each day unusable — the layout does not degrade gracefully mobile-first.
findings.recommendation: Keep 7 columns (calendar semantics require it) but make the whole grid fluid and legible: (1) set the container to allow horizontal breathing room and reduce cell chrome at small widths; (2) replace the single 768px breakpoint with a mobile-first ramp — base .calendar-day{ min-height: clamp(56px, 18vw, 120px) } so cell height tracks viewport, and .day-number{ font-size: clamp(0.7rem, 2.5vw, 1rem) }; (3) for the text tile use span{ font-size: clamp(0.6rem, 1.8vw, 0.72rem); -webkit-line-clamp: 2 } below ~400px; (4) if the owner wants true small-screen usability, wrap .calendar-grid in an overflow-x:auto scroller with min-width:420px so the week never crushes below a readable size. Prefer clamp() over the isolated 768px media query.

findings.title: Global body font-size is a fixed 18px and all headings scale in rem off the root, not fluidly — display headings overflow narrow viewports
findings.evidence: body { font-size: 18px; } (line 42) and h1 { font-size: 3rem; } h2 { font-size: 2.25rem; } (lines 64-68). Detail page uses <h1 class="display-4 ..."> and home uses class="display-5". With letter-spacing:-1px and long titles, a 3rem/Bootstrap display-4 heading on a 320px screen will overflow or wrap awkwardly. Nothing uses clamp() or viewport-relative type anywhere in the codebase.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte flexible typography / fluid layout: type should scale with the viewport (or at minimum use relative units that respond) so headings never overrun the fluid grid. Fixed px body type and static rem headings are non-fluid — they ignore available width.
findings.recommendation: Introduce fluid type in style.css: body { font-size: clamp(1rem, 0.95rem + 0.4vw, 1.125rem); } and replace static heading sizes with clamps, e.g. h1{ font-size: clamp(1.9rem, 1.4rem + 3.5vw, 3rem); } h2{ font-size: clamp(1.6rem, 1.2rem + 2.5vw, 2.25rem); } h3{ font-size: clamp(1.35rem, 1.1rem + 1.5vw, 1.75rem); }. For the display-4/display-5 titles in challenge_detail.html:30 and home.html:97, add a class that applies clamp() and also relax letter-spacing at small sizes (letter-spacing: clamp(-0.02em, -0.5px, 0)). This eliminates overflow while preserving the editorial look at desktop.

findings.title: Interpreter editor/console use fixed pixel heights (min-height:400px, max-height:600px, min-height:120px) that do not adapt when the two columns stack on mobile
findings.evidence: Editor pane: style="...min-height: 400px;" and .CodeMirror { height:100%; min-height:400px }. Output pane: style="max-height: 600px; overflow-y: auto;" with console min-height:120px. The card-body uses d-flex flex-column flex-lg-row, so below 992px the editor (400px min) stacks ON TOP of the output (up to 600px) — a phone user must scroll ~1000px+ of fixed interpreter chrome before reaching tests/comments.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte flexible layout + mobile-first: component dimensions should be proportional/viewport-aware, not fixed px, especially once a two-column layout collapses to a single stacked column on small screens. Fixed heights create excessive vertical scroll and wasted space on mobile.
findings.recommendation: Make the interpreter heights fluid and breakpoint-aware. In the @media (min-width:992px) block keep the current fixed heights (they read well side-by-side); for the mobile-first base, set the editor min-height: clamp(220px, 45vh, 400px) and the output pane max-height: clamp(240px, 50vh, 600px). Also give .CodeMirror min-height: clamp(220px, 45vh, 400px) so it matches. This keeps a usable editor on desktop while cutting the stacked mobile height roughly in half.

findings.title: Hand-rolled .w-lg-50 utility duplicates Bootstrap and is defined per-page instead of once — brittle two-column interpreter split
findings.evidence: .w-lg-50 { width: 100%; } @media (min-width: 992px) { .w-lg-50 { width: 50% !important; } } — a custom reimplementation of a responsive width utility, hardcoded to the 992px lg breakpoint, living inside a single template's extra_css. The markup pairs it with Bootstrap's own w-100 (class="w-100 w-lg-50").
findings.methodologyPrinciple: CSS Grid/Flex best practice + DRY: responsive splits should use the framework's grid (Bootstrap .row/.col-lg-6) or a modern intrinsic layout (flex: 1 1 0 / grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(...))) rather than a page-local px-breakpoint hack that can drift from the rest of the responsive system.
findings.recommendation: Replace the .w-lg-50 panes with Bootstrap's grid so the two columns are governed by the same breakpoint system as the rest of the app: wrap the two panes in <div class="row g-0"> and use <div class="col-12 col-lg-6">, dropping the custom .w-lg-50 block entirely. Alternatively, keep flex but make it intrinsic: on the card-body use display:flex; flex-wrap:wrap and give each pane flex: 1 1 min(100%, 28rem) so they split when there is room and stack otherwise — a container-query-style behavior without a hardcoded 992px value. If the utility is kept, hoist it into style.css so it is defined once.

findings.title: Lightbox modal uses fixed pixel offsets and padding that overflow / crowd small screens
findings.evidence: #modalClose { position: fixed; top: 20px; right: 40px; font-size: 40px; } #modalWrapper { padding: 50px 20px 20px 20px; } .modal-text { max-width: 760px; width:100%; padding: 40px 36px; }. The 40px close glyph at right:40px can overlap the .modal-text card on a 320px screen, and 40px 36px card padding leaves little content width on a phone.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte flexible layout / flexible images: modal chrome and padding should scale with the viewport so the close affordance and content never collide or eat the limited mobile width. Fixed px offsets are non-fluid.
findings.recommendation: Fluidize the modal: #modalClose { top: clamp(10px, 2.5vw, 20px); right: clamp(14px, 4vw, 40px); font-size: clamp(28px, 7vw, 40px); }. #modalWrapper { padding: clamp(48px,10vw,50px) clamp(12px,4vw,20px) 20px; }. .modal-text { padding: clamp(20px, 5vw, 40px) clamp(16px, 5vw, 36px); }. This preserves the desktop look while guaranteeing the close button and card body fit within a 320px viewport without overlap.

findings.title: Solution code block on detail page has no responsive overflow guard for long lines beyond vertical scroll
findings.evidence:
The 'Actual Final Code' card-body has style="max-height: 600px; overflow-y: auto;" and the 
{{ challenge.solution_code }}
only inherits overflow-x from .markdown-content pre { overflow-x: auto } — but this solution pre is NOT inside .markdown-content (it is a raw pre in the code card), so it has no overflow-x rule. A long Python line pushes the page wider than the viewport, producing horizontal page scroll on mobile.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte flexible layout: no element should force the document wider than the viewport. Wide code must be contained (overflow-x on the pre), not allowed to stretch the fluid grid horizontally.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit rule for the solution pre, e.g. in the detail page style block: .card-body pre { overflow-x: auto; max-width: 100%; } (or scope it: #solutionCode's parent pre). Also consider white-space handling: keeping overflow-x:auto preserves code formatting while confining the scroll to the box. This is the same protection already applied to .markdown-content pre (lines 292-298) — extend it to the standalone code card.

findings.title: Satisfaction emoji row uses fixed large gap (gap-4, fs-3) and can wrap poorly / crowd on narrow screens; hover transform:scale enlarges without reserved space
findings.evidence: <div class="d-flex justify-content-center gap-4 fs-3"> with five emoji <span> each using onmouseover="this.style.transform='scale(1.2)'". gap-4 (1.5rem) between five fs-3 emojis is ~ fine at desktop but on a 320px screen five ~1.75rem glyphs + 4×1.5rem gaps ≈ 240px+ leaves almost no margin; there is no flex-wrap, so it can overflow the card padding.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte fluid layout + flexible spacing: inter-element spacing should shrink on small viewports; a fixed 1.5rem gap combined with fixed glyph size is non-fluid and risks overflow of a horizontal control.
findings.recommendation: Add flex-wrap and a fluid gap: change to class="d-flex flex-wrap justify-content-center fs-3" style="gap: clamp(0.5rem, 4vw, 1.5rem);". Emoji touch targets are also small for fingers — consider padding each span (padding:0.25rem) so the tap area meets ~44px. This keeps the desktop spacing while preventing overflow on phones.

findings.title: Archive Flatpickr wrapper reserves a fixed 380px min-height and the picker itself is not width-constrained for very small screens
findings.evidence: <div class="d-flex justify-content-center mx-auto" style="min-height: 380px;"> containing #inlineDatePicker. The inline Flatpickr renders at its intrinsic width; the fixed 380px min-height reserves desktop-sized vertical space even on phones where the inline calendar is shorter, and there is no max-width guard to prevent the picker's default ~307px table from crowding a 320px viewport.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte fluid layout: reserved dimensions should be viewport-aware; a fixed min-height creates dead space on small screens (mobile-first would set the smaller value as the base).
findings.recommendation: Change to min-height: clamp(300px, 60vw, 380px) and add #inlineDatePicker .flatpickr-calendar { max-width: 100%; } so the inline archive calendar fits within the column on the narrowest phones without introducing horizontal scroll. Low priority since Flatpickr's own CSS is mostly responsive.

findings.title: Footer tagline is a long single-line paragraph in col-md-8 with fixed 0.85rem and no small-screen treatment; copyright col-md-4 stacks without spacing tuning
findings.evidence:
Transportation Specialist at @amzn | ... | Mechanical Engineer

and footer{ padding: var(--spacing-md) 0 } (16px). On mobile the long pipe-delimited tagline wraps to 3-4 lines at fixed 0.85rem with only 16px vertical footer padding, feeling cramped against the viewport edges (no container gutter increase).
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Marcotte fluid layout / mobile-first: dense text blocks should get slightly more breathing room and controlled type on small screens; static px font + minimal padding is not tuned for the stacked mobile layout.
findings.recommendation: Minor: give the footer fluid vertical padding footer{ padding: clamp(16px, 4vw, 24px) 0 } and set the tagline font-size: clamp(0.75rem, 2.5vw, 0.85rem). Optionally hide the pipe separators and use a flex-wrapped list with gaps for cleaner wrapping. Cosmetic, lowest priority.

findings.title: No component-level responsiveness (container queries) for the reusable card/interpreter components — all breakpoints are viewport-global
findings.evidence: Every responsive decision keys off viewport width (Bootstrap col-lg-*, the single @media (max-width:768px) in challenges.html, the @media (min-width:992px) w-lg-50 rule). style.css contains zero @media and zero @container rules. The two-column interpreter and the concept/code cards would benefit from adapting to their own container width (e.g., inside a future narrower layout) rather than the viewport.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Modern CSS Grid/Flex best practice: prefer container queries (@container) for component-level responsiveness so reusable components (cards, the interpreter) adapt to the space they are given, decoupled from the global viewport. The codebase relies exclusively on viewport media queries.
findings.recommendation: For the interpreter card, opt into container queries: on the card-body set container-type: inline-size, then @container (min-width: 720px){ .interpreter-pane{ width:50% } } instead of the viewport-based .w-lg-50. Baseline browser support for container queries is broad as of 2026; keep the Bootstrap grid as the primary system but adopt @container for the interpreter and detail two-column split so they respond to their own width. Forward-looking / lowest priority.

topPriorities: R-01 (high): Calendar grid is hard-locked to repeat(7, 1fr) with only a single 768px media query — cells collapse to ~40px and text tiles become unreadable on phones. Add fluid clamp() cell sizing and an overflow-x scroller fallback so the week never crushes below a legible width.

R-02 (high): No fluid typography anywhere — body is a fixed 18px and headings are static rem (h1:3rem, display-4/5) that overflow 320px viewports. Introduce clamp()-based type for body and all headings in style.css.

R-03 (medium): Interpreter editor/console use fixed 400px/600px/120px heights that produce ~1000px of stacked chrome on mobile. Switch the mobile-first base to clamp(vh) heights, keep fixed px only inside the >=992px media query.
R-05 (medium): Lightbox modal fixed px offsets (close button right:40px/40px glyph, .modal-text 40px 36px padding, 760px max-width) crowd/overlap on small screens — fluidize with clamp().
audits.summary: The app has a correct viewport meta tag (base.html:6) and leans on Bootstrap 5's grid for page-level layout, which handles the navbar (collapse toggler), register/verify/dashboard column stacking, and home/detail col-lg-6 splits acceptably. However, measured against Marcotte's mobile-first fluid methodology and modern CSS Grid/Flex practice, the custom CSS is almost entirely NON-fluid: there is zero use of clamp() and only two isolated media queries (one 768px in challenges.html, one 992px .w-lg-50 hack in challenge_detail.html), while style.css contains no @media rules at all. The two most impactful issues are the calendar grid (repeat(7,1fr) hard-locked with no column/size adaptation, so cells collapse to ~40px on phones — R-01) and the absence of fluid typography (fixed 18px body, static 3rem/display-4 headings that overflow narrow screens — R-02). Secondary issues cluster around fixed pixel dimensions that ignore the viewport once layouts stack: the interpreter's 400/600/120px heights (R-03), the modal's fixed close-button offset and card padding (R-05), and a standalone code <pre> missing overflow-x that lets long lines stretch the whole document (R-06). The .w-lg-50 utility (R-04) is a page-local reimplementation of Bootstrap responsive widths hardcoded to 992px and should be replaced with the grid or an intrinsic flex/container-query split. None of the findings are server-only — all are pure client-side CSS/template edits, and the highest-value fixes (R-02, R-03, R-05, R-06) are small/trivial and safe. The recommended direction throughout is mobile-first: set the smaller value as the base and scale UP via clamp()/minmax() rather than patching down from desktop with max-width media queries.

audits.methodology: Core Web Vitals (LCP / CLS / INP) + RAIL performance model + PRPL pattern + Google Web.dev image optimization best practices (responsive images, modern formats, explicit dimensions, lazy-loading, efficient cache policy, text compression). Each finding cites the specific principle it violates.

findings.title: 150 challenge JPGs render with no width/height and no aspect-ratio => guaranteed CLS on the calendar
findings.evidence: challenges.html:341 `` — no width/height/loading/decoding attributes anywhere (grep of templates/ returned only the viewport meta). Images are 900x1200 (portrait, measured with PIL). The thumb is `position:absolute; object-fit:contain` inside a 120px min-height cell, so the cell itself is height-constrained; but the browser still has zero intrinsic dimension info at parse time, and any image that fails/loads late reflows the absolutely-positioned layer.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Core Web Vitals — CLS: 'Always include width and height size attributes on images and video elements' (web.dev/optimize-cls). Absent intrinsic dimensions the browser cannot reserve space, producing layout shift.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit `width="900" height="1200"` (or a downscaled 300x400 pair) to every `` in challenges.html:341 and home.html:55. Because all images share the 900x1200 aspect ratio this is a static edit. Additionally set `aspect-ratio: 3 / 4` on `.challenge-thumb` in the CSS block (challenges.html:82) so the reserved box is correct even before the bitmap arrives. This eliminates the reflow of the absolutely-positioned thumbnail layer.

findings.title: Calendar loads all ~30-31 same-month thumbnails eagerly; no loading=lazy / decoding=async
findings.evidence: challenges.html:308 `{% for month_data in calendar_data[:1] %}` renders the most recent month's grid; each day with a manual image emits an eager `` (line 341) with no `loading="lazy"`. Manual-source challenges are ~130-200KB JPGs (measured max 200150 bytes). A full month of manual uploads = up to ~30 images x ~150KB ≈ 4.5MB fetched on first paint, all at default (eager) priority, competing with the LCP image and render-blocking CSS.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Google image best practices / PRPL — 'Lazy-load offscreen images' and 'Push/preload only what's critical, lazily load the rest.' Below-the-fold calendar cells should not contend for bandwidth during initial load.
findings.recommendation: Add `loading="lazy" decoding="async"` to the calendar `` (challenges.html:341). Keep the first visible row eager if it is the LCP candidate. For the home page hero thumbnail (home.html:55), instead add `fetchpriority="high"` since it IS the LCP element and must not be lazied.

findings.title: Images served as unoptimized 900x1200 JPG (~130-200KB) for a ~120px calendar cell — 10-40x oversized, no modern format
locations: static/images/*.jpg (150 files, 22MB total, up to 200KB each, all 900x1200)
findings.evidence: Measured: `20250909_unique_characters.jpg` = 900x1200 @ 127KB; largest = 200150 bytes; total `static/images` = 22MB across 150 files. The calendar cell is `min-height:120px` in a 7-column grid (each cell ~110-160px wide on desktop). A 900px-wide source is decoded for a ~112px display box => ~8x linear / ~64x pixel over-fetch. No WebP/AVIF variants exist; format is baseline JPG.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Google image best practices — 'Serve images with correct dimensions' (avoid `Properly size images` Lighthouse audit) and 'Serve images in next-gen formats (WebP/AVIF)'. Oversized/legacy-format images inflate LCP and transfer bytes.
findings.recommendation: Generate two derivatives per image at build/upload time (extend image_processor.py, which already uses OpenCV/Pillow): a 300x400 thumbnail and an 800px full view, each in WebP (quality ~80). Serve via `<picture>` with WebP + JPG fallback, or `srcset`/`sizes` (e.g. `sizes="(max-width:768px) 30vw, 150px"`) so the browser picks the thumbnail on the calendar and the full image only in the lightbox modal. Expected transfer reduction >90% on the calendar.

findings.title: Static assets served by Flask/gunicorn with no Cache-Control / no compression config
locations: app.py (no SEND_FILE_MAX_AGE_DEFAULT, no after_request cache headers, no Flask-Compress — grep found none)
requirements.txt (no Flask-Compress; no gunicorn conf file present)
findings.evidence: grep for `SEND_FILE_MAX_AGE|Cache-Control|max_age|after_request|Compress|gzip` across all *.py returned nothing. No nginx/gunicorn conf file exists (ls found none). Flask's dev static handler and gunicorn serve these with default headers; the 22MB of images and style.css are re-downloaded on every visit with no far-future caching and no gzip/brotli on text assets.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Core Web Vitals / Lighthouse — 'Serve static assets with an efficient cache policy' and 'Enable text compression'. Immutable fingerprinted assets should have long max-age; text (CSS/HTML/JSON) should be compressed.
findings.recommendation: Front the app with nginx (already referenced in watchdog) or add app-level headers: set `SEND_FILE_MAX_AGE_DEFAULT` and an `@app.after_request` adding `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` for `/static/images/*` (they never change once uploaded). Add gzip/brotli — either nginx `gzip on` for text/*, or `Flask-Compress` for the HTML/JSON responses (the /api/challenge JSON and markdown-heavy pages compress well). Add cache-busting query strings when an image is re-uploaded.

findings.title: challenge_detail loads Pyodide (~heavy WASM) + CodeMirror + confetti eagerly on page load, not on demand
findings.evidence: challenge_detail.html:413 `pyodide = await loadPyodide();` runs unconditionally inside the `DOMContentLoaded` handler for every detail-page visit. `pyodide.js` (line 390) bootstraps a multi-MB CPython WASM runtime plus its packages; CodeMirror + python mode + monokai theme + confetti are all fetched from CDNs on load. The interpreter is far below the fold (after problem, concepts, code, Q&A). Most visitors read the solution and never type code, yet pay the full WASM download/instantiation cost, saturating the main thread and network during initial render.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: PRPL — 'lazily load routes/features on demand'; RAIL — keep the main thread free during Load so the page is interactive fast. Eagerly instantiating a WASM VM no visitor asked for violates both.
findings.recommendation: Defer Pyodide/CodeMirror until the user intends to code: (1) mark the three script tags `defer`; (2) do NOT call `loadPyodide()` in DOMContentLoaded — trigger it on first interaction, e.g. an IntersectionObserver on the interpreter card, or on first click of `#runCodeBtn`/`#runTestsBtn`/first focus of the editor. Show a 'Loading Python runtime…' state while it initializes. Lazy-init CodeMirror the same way. This removes multi-MB of blocking work from the LCP/INP path for the majority who only read.

findings.title: bootstrap.bundle.js is a plain end-of-body <script> without defer; CodeMirror/Pyodide scripts also parser-block
findings.evidence: base.html:86 `<script src="...bootstrap.bundle.min.js"></script>` — no `defer`. Although at end of body, without `defer` it still blocks the parser at that point and executes synchronously before the closing tags/inline scripts. challenge_detail.html:386-390 load CodeMirror + Pyodide as classic blocking scripts; challenges.html:424 loads flatpickr blocking. None use `defer` or `async`.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Core Web Vitals INP / RAIL — minimize main-thread blocking; PRPL — non-critical JS should not block parse/render. `defer` lets the parser finish and runs scripts in order after DOM is ready.
findings.recommendation: Add `defer` to base.html:86 (bootstrap), challenges.html:424 (flatpickr), and challenge_detail.html:386-387 (CodeMirror). Keep dependency order (defer preserves it). This shortens the time to first interactive and reduces INP contributions from synchronous parse/eval.

findings.title: Missing preconnect/dns-prefetch to the 3 third-party CDNs used on every page (jsdelivr, cdnjs, pyodide)
findings.evidence: base.html:13-14 preconnects only to `fonts.googleapis.com` and `fonts.gstatic.com`. Bootstrap CSS (jsdelivr, line 10) is render-blocking yet has no preconnect. The detail page pulls from `cdnjs.cloudflare.com` (CodeMirror) and `cdn.jsdelivr.net/pyodide` (line 390) with no preconnect — each incurs a fresh DNS+TLS+TCP handshake on the critical path.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: PRPL / web.dev 'Preconnect to required origins' — establishing early connections to critical third-party origins removes handshake latency from the resource-fetch critical path, improving LCP.
findings.recommendation: In base.html <head> add `<link rel="preconnect" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net" crossorigin>` (bootstrap CSS is render-blocking so this helps LCP directly). On challenge_detail.html add preconnects to `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com`. Optionally self-host Bootstrap/CodeMirror to remove third-party dependency entirely.

findings.title: bootstrap-icons CSS loaded at END of body — late-loaded render-blocking stylesheet causes icon FOUT/CLS
locations: templates/base.html:89 (<link rel="stylesheet" bootstrap-icons ... after </footer>, inside body)
findings.evidence: base.html:89 places `<link rel="stylesheet" href="...bootstrap-icons@1.11.0/font/bootstrap-icons.css">` in the body, after the footer and the bootstrap bundle script. Icon glyphs (`bi bi-*`) are used heavily in the navbar (base.html:38) and throughout pages above this link. A stylesheet discovered late means icons are unstyled until it loads, then swap in — an icon-font FOUT and potential shift.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Core Web Vitals CLS + web.dev 'Avoid non-composited/late CSS' — stylesheets belong in <head> so the browser discovers and applies them before painting the content that depends on them; late CSS causes reflow/FOUT.
findings.recommendation: Move the bootstrap-icons <link> from base.html:89 into <head> (near line 10). Add `<link rel="preconnect">` for its origin (covered by PERF-07). If icon flash persists, preload the woff2 with `<link rel="preload" as="font" crossorigin>`.

findings.title: Google Fonts request pulls 4 families x many weights render-blocking; no font preload, no self-host
locations: templates/base.html:15-17 (Crimson Text + Libre Franklin + Inter + JetBrains Mono, ~14 weight/style combos)
findings.evidence: base.html:16 requests `Crimson+Text` (5 variants incl italics) + `Libre+Franklin` (5 weights) + `Inter` (4 weights) + `JetBrains+Mono` (2 weights) in one render-blocking stylesheet. That is ~16 font files. `display=swap` is present (good, avoids invisible text) but swap itself causes a font-swap layout shift for the serif body text, and the large family set delays first meaningful paint. Inter is requested but the CSS variables (style.css:23-24) only reference Crimson Text and Libre Franklin — Inter/JetBrains may be largely unused as body text.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Core Web Vitals LCP/CLS + web.dev 'Optimize WebFont loading' — reduce the number of font resources, preload the LCP font, and prefer self-hosting to cut a blocking third-party round trip. Excess weights inflate the critical path.
findings.recommendation: Trim to the weights actually rendered (audit usage; drop unused Inter/JetBrains variants). Preload the primary above-the-fold font (Crimson Text 400/700 woff2) with `<link rel="preload" as="font" crossorigin>`. Consider self-hosting subsetted woff2 to eliminate the fonts.googleapis blocking request. Keep `display=swap` but pair with `size-adjust`/fallback metrics to minimize the swap shift.

findings.title: Per-request server-side markdown rendering with no caching; recomputed on every home/detail/api hit
findings.evidence: app.py:215-217 `problem_html = markdown.markdown(challenge.problem_text or "")` etc. run on every GET of a detail page; app.py:426-430 re-render markdown on every `/api/challenge/<id>` call (fired by every calendar-modal open). The source markdown only changes when content is edited (admin/sync), yet it is re-parsed on every request. Same for the FCC test regex parsing at app.py:222-241 (json.loads + re.search per test, every request). This is repeated CPU on a single self-hosted laptop under gunicorn.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: RAIL 'Response' budget (<100ms server work to feel instant) — avoid recomputing deterministic, cacheable output per request; precompute or cache derived content.
findings.recommendation: Render markdown once at write time: add nullable `problem_html`/`concepts_html`/`qa_html` columns (append-only rule permits ADD nullable) and populate them in `_handle_admin_post` and `fcc_sync` upsert; templates then read the stored HTML. Alternatively wrap `markdown.markdown` and the FCC-test parse in `functools.lru_cache` keyed on `(challenge.id, challenge.updated_at)`. Removes redundant per-request CPU and speeds TTFB (LCP contributor).

findings.title: challenges() loads ALL challenges and does O(months x challenges) date-parsing to render only one month
locations: app.py:179-200 (nested loop re-parsing every date_id per month)
findings.evidence: app.py:166 fetches every challenge row; app.py:184 re-iterates ALL challenges inside the per-month loop (`for c in all_challenges` nested under `for year,month`), calling `datetime.strptime` on every date_id for every month => O(months x rows). But the template (challenges.html:308 `calendar_data[:1]`) renders only the newest month as a grid; the rest feed only Flatpickr's min/max. So the server builds ~10 month matrices and parses ~150 dates ~10 times that are never rendered as grids.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: RAIL 'Response' — do not perform work whose output is discarded; minimize server compute on the critical path to keep TTFB low.
findings.recommendation: Parse each date_id once (build a `{(y,m): {day: challenge}}` dict in a single pass), and only build the matrix for the newest month the template actually renders. Flatpickr only needs min/max dates, which are the first/last date_id from the already-ordered query — no per-month matrix needed. Cuts the /challenges handler work substantially.

findings.title: home() issues redundant queries and an uncached synchronous FCC network fetch risk on the LCP path
findings.evidence: home() runs `Challenge.query.order_by(...).first()` (line 123) and `Challenge.query.count()` (line 127) as two round trips. `get_daily_quote()` (line 142) is called during the request; on an hourly cache miss it makes a synchronous `requests.get(...timeout=5)` to GitHub (app.py:305) inline in the request — up to a 5s stall on the home-page TTFB once per hour, directly blocking LCP.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: RAIL 'Response' / Core Web Vitals LCP — synchronous third-party network I/O inside a page request can add seconds to TTFB; server work should be bounded and off the critical path.
findings.recommendation: Move the quote refresh off the request path: refresh the quote cache from the existing cron/watchdog (fcc_sync) or a background thread, and have home() only read the cached file (never fetch inline). This caps the worst-case home TTFB regardless of GitHub latency. Minor: the count query is cheap but could be combined/cached.

topPriorities: PERF-05: Stop eagerly instantiating the Pyodide WASM VM + CodeMirror + confetti on every challenge_detail visit — lazy-load them on first user intent (IntersectionObserver / first editor click). This is the single largest wasted download and main-thread cost, paid by the majority who only read the solution (PRPL + INP/LCP).

PERF-03 + PERF-01 + PERF-02: Fix the image story on the calendar — serve resized WebP thumbnails via srcset (currently 900x1200 ~150KB JPGs for a ~112px cell), add explicit width/height + aspect-ratio to kill CLS, and add loading=lazy/decoding=async. Together these are the biggest LCP + CLS + bytes wins for the /challenges page.

PERF-04: Add far-future Cache-Control (immutable) for /static/images and enable gzip/brotli — 22MB of images currently re-fetch with no cache policy and text assets are uncompressed (efficient cache policy + text compression).

PERF-06 + PERF-07 + PERF-08: Cheap, safe head-of-line wins — defer bootstrap/flatpickr/CodeMirror scripts, preconnect to jsdelivr/cdnjs, and move the bootstrap-icons stylesheet from end-of-body into <head>.

PERF-10 + PERF-12: Remove per-request server work from the critical path — cache/precompute markdown HTML (rendered every request today) and take the synchronous FCC quote fetch off the home-page request (up to 5s TTFB stall on cache miss).
audits.summary: The app is functionally solid but ships with essentially zero front-end performance discipline against Core Web Vitals / RAIL / PRPL. The dominant issues cluster on two pages. (1) The calendar (/challenges) renders up to ~30 manual-upload thumbnails as full-resolution 900x1200 JPGs (~130-200KB each, 22MB/150 files total) into ~112px cells, with no width/height, no aspect-ratio, no loading=lazy, no modern format, and no cache/compression policy — a compound LCP + CLS + bytes problem (PERF-01/02/03/04). (2) The challenge detail page eagerly boots a multi-MB Pyodide WASM runtime plus CodeMirror and confetti on every visit inside DOMContentLoaded, regardless of whether the visitor ever writes code — the biggest single main-thread/network waste and a clear PRPL/INP violation (PERF-05). Layered on top are cheap head-of-line fixes: no defer on bootstrap/flatpickr/CodeMirror scripts, missing preconnect to the three CDNs, and the bootstrap-icons stylesheet loaded at end-of-body (PERF-06/07/08), plus an over-broad Google Fonts request (PERF-09). Server-side, markdown is re-rendered per request (including on every calendar-modal /api/challenge call), /challenges builds ~10 month matrices it never renders, and the home page can make a synchronous 5s FCC fetch on the request path (PERF-10/11/12) — all TTFB/LCP contributors on a single self-hosted laptop. Highest-leverage, lowest-risk sequence: lazy-load Pyodide, ship resized WebP thumbnails with dimensions + lazy-loading, add cache/compression headers, then the trivial defer/preconnect/head-move edits. Note several fixes (image derivatives, cache headers, markdown precompute) are server-side and cannot be validated in this local read-only environment.

audits.methodology: SOLID (Single Responsibility, Dependency Inversion) + DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) + Separation of Concerns + Flask Application Factory pattern (create_app + Blueprints, per the official Flask "Larger Applications" / "Application Factories" patterns)

findings.title: home() fabricates statistics: mastered=total, in_progress=1 (hardcoded), and the entire stats dict is never rendered
findings.evidence: app.py:127-137 — `total_challenges = Challenge.query.count()` / `mastered = total_challenges` / `in_progress = 1` / `progress_percent: round((mastered / total_challenges) * 100, 1)`. This asserts every challenge is 'mastered' and hardcodes one 'in_progress'. `progress_percent` is therefore always 100.0 (mastered==total). A grep of templates/home.html for `stats|mastered|progress|percent|total` returns zero matches — the dict is computed, passed to render_template, and never used.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Single Responsibility Principle + Separation of Concerns: a view function should compute values that reflect real state, not fabricate business metrics inline. Also violates DRY/dead-code hygiene (YAGNI): computing and passing data no consumer reads.
findings.recommendation: Delete lines 127-138 and remove `stats=stats` from the render_template call (app.py:153). If a stats banner is genuinely desired later, derive counts from a real source of truth (e.g. a `status`/`source` column) in a dedicated helper like `compute_challenge_stats()` in a service module — never `mastered = total` and never a literal `in_progress = 1`. Until then, do not fabricate.

findings.title: Duplicated markdown-rendering logic across four routes (no shared helper)
findings.evidence: The same `markdown.markdown(<field> or "")` conversion is repeated in home() (problem_text, concepts_text), challenge_detail() (problem_text, concepts_text, qa_text), and api_challenge() (problem_text, concepts_text). Four routes independently decide how a Challenge's text fields become HTML, with subtly inconsistent guards: home()/challenge_detail() use `or ""`, while api_challenge() uses ternaries `markdown.markdown(c.concepts_text) if c.concepts_text else ""` — same intent, three different spellings.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: DRY: the rule 'how a Challenge renders its markdown to HTML' is expressed 7+ times. A single change to rendering (e.g. adding markdown extensions, sanitizing output, or the fcc_description-vs-problem_text precedence) must currently be hand-edited in multiple places, inviting drift (already visible in the inconsistent guards).
findings.recommendation: Add rendering to the model or a small service: e.g. on Challenge a property `problem_html`/`concepts_html`/`qa_html` returning `markdown.markdown(self.problem_text or '')`, plus a `display_description_html` property that encodes the fcc_description-else-problem_text precedence used in api_challenge(). Routes then read `challenge.problem_html`. This centralizes the rule and lets the api_challenge and home precedence logic share one implementation.

findings.title: get_fcc_quote() and get_daily_quote() duplicate the FCC motivation.json fetch
findings.evidence: Both functions hardcode the identical URL `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/freeCodeCamp/freeCodeCamp/main/client/i18n/locales/english/motivation.json`, both do `requests.get(..., timeout=5)`, both check `status_code == 200`, both `random.choice(data.get('motivationalQuotes', []))`, and both fall back to the same Lincoln quote. The only real difference is the return shape: get_fcc_quote returns a preformatted string `f'"{q}"\n- {a}'` for the admin form; get_daily_quote returns a `(quote, author)` tuple with an hourly disk cache.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: DRY: the network fetch + parse + fallback is copied verbatim in two functions. If the FCC JSON schema, URL, or timeout changes, both must be edited. Also Single Responsibility: quote-fetching, caching, and formatting are conflated.
findings.recommendation: Extract one private `_fetch_random_fcc_quote() -> tuple[str, str]` that does the network+parse+fallback. get_daily_quote() wraps it with the hourly cache and returns the tuple; get_fcc_quote() becomes `q, a = get_daily_quote(); return f'"{q}"\n- {a}'` (or calls the extracted fetch directly). Removes ~15 duplicated lines and makes the admin quote benefit from the same cache.

findings.title: Redundant function-local imports of already-module-imported modules
findings.evidence: `import markdown` and `import requests` are already at module top (app.py:9-10), yet home() re-imports markdown at line 140 and rate_challenge() re-imports requests at line 624. `import re` is done inline at line 219 inside challenge_detail() only. These serve no purpose (Python caches modules in sys.modules) and signal copy-paste assembly.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Separation of Concerns / code hygiene: imports belong at module scope so dependencies are declared once and discoverable. Inline re-imports are dead noise that obscure the real dependency surface and violate DRY (the import statement itself is duplicated).
findings.recommendation: Delete `import markdown` (line 140) and `import requests` (line 624). Move `import re` to the module-level import block at the top of app.py and delete line 219. No behavior change.

findings.title: 650-line single-file app mixes config, DB engine tuning, business logic, external HTTP, and 20+ routes — no app-factory, no blueprints
locations: app.py:55-81 (engine options + pragma event listener at module scope)
app.py:117-645 (all routes: public, auth, admin, api, oauth stubs)
findings.evidence: A single module holds: env/config (28-42), SQLite engine options + a global `@event.listens_for(Engine,'connect')` pragma listener (55-81), a filesystem loader `load_challenges()` (85-114, apparently unused by routes), markdown business logic, quote-fetch/caching, admin upload handling, and every route across four unrelated concerns (content pages, mock-OTP auth, unauthenticated admin, JSON API, OAuth stubs). `app` is created at module import (line 26), so config cannot vary per environment (test vs prod) and importing app.py always spins up a full app + registers a global engine listener.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Flask Application Factory pattern + Single Responsibility Principle + Separation of Concerns: Flask's documented guidance for anything beyond a toy is `create_app()` returning a configured app, with route groups split into Blueprints (content, auth, admin, api). The current file couples configuration to import time (blocking test configs / multiple instances) and gives one module ~six responsibilities.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a modest factory without over-engineering a small app: (1) `create_app(config=None)` that builds `app`, loads config, calls `db.init_app(app)`, `Migrate(app, db)`, registers the pragma listener, and registers blueprints. (2) Split routes into ~4 blueprints in a `views/` package: `main` (/, /challenges, /challenge/<id>, /sql), `auth` (register/verify_otp/dashboard/logout/oauth stubs), `admin` (/admin + helpers), `api` (/api/*). (3) Move `get_daily_quote`, markdown helpers, and quote-fetch into a `services.py`. (4) `wsgi.py`/entry does `app = create_app()`; keep `app.run` only under `if __name__=='__main__'`. This is 4 small files, not a framework — proportional to a ~20-route app.

findings.title: Monolithic templates with large inline <script> and <style> blocks duplicated across pages
