findings.evidence: challenge_detail.html is 629 lines with 6 inline <script> blocks and 2 inline <style> blocks (Pyodide runner, CodeMirror wiring, test logic all embedded); challenges.html is 613 lines with inline calendar JS/CSS; home.html and admin.html each carry their own inline <style>. base.html hardcodes an anti-copy `contextmenu`/`onkeydown` blocker inline (91-112). Presentation, structure, and client behavior are interleaved per template rather than extracted.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Separation of Concerns: markup (structure), CSS (presentation), and JS (behavior) should be separable; large inline blocks prevent caching, reuse, and testing. DRY: per-page inline <style> blocks re-declare styling that a shared stylesheet (static/css/style.css already exists) should own.
findings.recommendation: Move page-specific JS into `static/js/challenge_detail.js`, `static/js/challenges.js` referenced via `{% block extra_js %}<script src=...>`; move page <style> blocks into namespaced sections of static/css/style.css (or per-page CSS files) loaded via `{% block extra_css %}`. Keep only truly dynamic, Jinja-interpolated values inline (e.g. a small `<script>window.CHALLENGE = {{ ... | tojson }}</script>` data island) and pull static logic out. This also lets the browser cache the JS/CSS across page loads.

findings.title: load_challenges() is dead code: a full filesystem-based content loader no route calls
findings.evidence: `load_challenges()` scans UPLOAD_FOLDER for .jpg files, parses `YYYYMMDD_name.jpg`, and builds challenge dicts with a `status: 'not_started'` field. No route in app.py calls it (challenges/home/detail all query the DB via SQLAlchemy). It is a leftover from a pre-DB filesystem design and its `status` field is the likely origin of the phantom 'mastered/in_progress' vocabulary in CL-1.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: DRY / YAGNI / Separation of Concerns: two competing sources of truth for challenges (filesystem parse vs DB) are described in one module; the unused one is dead weight that misleads readers about how content flows.
findings.recommendation: Delete `load_challenges()` (app.py:85-114). If filename-parsing is still needed anywhere (it does not appear to be), move it to the ingestion layer (fcc_sync/image pipeline), not the web app.

findings.title: Business rules embedded in view functions instead of the model/service layer (rating classification, OAuth scope, quote-precedence)
locations: app.py:630 (`flag_status = 'Green Flag' if rating in ['🤩','🙂'] else 'Red Flag'`)
findings.evidence: rate_challenge() hardcodes the emoji-to-flag mapping inline; api_challenge() encodes description-source precedence inline (which home()/challenge_detail() do NOT share, see CL-2); github() hardcodes OAuth scope in the route. These are business/domain rules living in HTTP handlers.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Separation of Concerns + Single Responsibility: view functions should orchestrate request/response, delegating domain decisions (what counts as a positive rating, which text to display, what OAuth scopes to request) to a service/model layer so the rules are testable and reused.
findings.recommendation: Move rating classification into a small `classify_rating(rating) -> str` helper; move the description precedence into the Challenge.display_description_html property from CL-2 so all three consumers agree; lift OAuth scope to a config constant. Keeps routes thin and rules in one place.

topPriorities: CL-1: Remove the fabricated stats in home() (mastered=total, in_progress=1, always-100% progress) — it computes misleading business metrics that no template even renders; trivial delete.

CL-3 + CL-2: Collapse the duplicated FCC quote fetch (get_fcc_quote vs get_daily_quote) and the repeated markdown.markdown rendering into single helpers/model properties — highest DRY payoff, low risk.

CL-5: Adopt a modest Flask application-factory + ~4 blueprints (content/auth/admin/api) and a services.py, so the 650-line god-module stops coupling config to import time and mixing six concerns — do this incrementally after the cheap cleanups land.
CL-4 + CL-7: Delete redundant inline imports (markdown/requests/re) and the dead load_challenges() loader to reduce noise and eliminate the phantom status vocabulary feeding CL-1.
audits.summary: app.py is a 650-line single module that violates every methodology axis in the brief. Concrete confirmed defects, not speculation: (1) home() fabricates stats — mastered is set equal to total and in_progress is hardcoded to 1, making progress_percent permanently 100.0, and the entire stats dict is passed to home.html which never references it (verified by grep) — dead AND misleading. (2) Markdown rendering (markdown.markdown(field or '')) is duplicated across home(), challenge_detail(), and api_challenge() with three inconsistent null-guard spellings, and the fcc_description-vs-problem_text precedence lives only in api_challenge(), so the pages disagree. (3) get_fcc_quote() and get_daily_quote() copy the same FCC motivation.json URL, fetch, parse, and Lincoln fallback verbatim — the only difference is return shape and caching. (4) Redundant function-local re-imports of markdown (line 140), requests (line 624), and an inline import re (line 219) that are already/should be module-level. (5) No application factory: app = Flask(__name__) and a global engine pragma listener run at import time, and 20+ routes spanning content, mock-OTP auth, unauthenticated admin, JSON API, and OAuth stubs all live in one file — SRP/SoC and Flask's documented create_app + Blueprints pattern are both ignored. (6) Templates are monolithic (challenge_detail.html 629 lines with 6 inline scripts/2 styles; challenges.html 613 lines) mixing structure, presentation, and behavior, defeating caching and reuse. (7) load_challenges() (30 lines) is dead filesystem-loader code no route calls and is the likely origin of the phantom 'mastered/not_started' status vocabulary. Recommended path: start with the trivial safe deletions (CL-1, CL-4, CL-7), then the DRY consolidations (CL-2, CL-3, CL-8), then the larger structural factory/blueprint split (CL-5) and template asset extraction (CL-6) — a proportional refactor for a ~20-route app, not over-engineering.

audits.methodology: Codd's relational normal forms (1NF-3NF) — specifically Codd's definitions of candidate keys, primary keys, functional dependency, and the requirement that every non-key attribute be functionally dependent on "the whole key and nothing but the key" — combined with append-only migration discipline (schema evolves only by ADD table / ADD nullable column; migrations must be the single, complete source of truth for the deployed schema; never DROP).

findings.title: fcc_* columns, challenge_number, source, and 4 whole tables exist in the model but in NO migration — schema drift, migrations are not the source of truth
locations: portfolio-v2/models.py:41-53 (challenge_number, fcc_description, fcc_js_tests, fcc_py_tests, fcc_starter_js, fcc_starter_py, source)
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Append-only migration discipline: the migration chain must be the single, complete, replayable source of truth for the deployed schema. Here the Alembic head (49b711d2aba8) describes a challenges table with only 9 columns and no users/comments/concept_strengths/user_notebooks tables, while models.py declares 17 challenge columns + 4 more tables. The gap is bridged only by out-of-band db.create_all() calls, so a fresh `flask db upgrade` produces a schema that does NOT match the model. This is unmanaged drift.
findings.evidence: Initial migration: `sa.Column("image_path", ... nullable=False)` and columns stop at created_at/updated_at. Model adds `challenge_number`, `fcc_description`, `fcc_js_tests`, `fcc_py_tests`, `fcc_starter_js`, `fcc_starter_py`, `source` (models.py:41-53) that appear in no migration. fcc_sync.py:264-265 comment: `# Ensure all tables exist (handles new columns gracefully) / db.create_all()`.
findings.recommendation: Author ONE additive Alembic migration (down_revision=49b711d2aba8) that reconciles the deployed schema to the model, using idempotent guards so it is safe whether or not db.create_all() already ran. In upgrade(): inspect existing columns via `sa.inspect(op.get_bind())` and, for challenges, `batch_op.add_column` each of challenge_number/fcc_*/source only if absent; then `op.create_table` for users, comments, concept_strengths, user_notebooks guarded by `if not inspector.has_table(...)`. Make downgrade() a no-op `pass` (append-only forbids DROP). Then delete/quarantine the create_*_tables.py scripts and remove the db.create_all() call in fcc_sync.py:265 so migrations become the sole schema authority.

findings.title: db.create_all() cannot add columns to an existing table — the 'handles new columns gracefully' comment is false and hides drift
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Append-only migration discipline requires that additive column changes be applied by an explicit ALTER (migration). SQLAlchemy's create_all() only issues CREATE TABLE for tables that do not yet exist; it never emits ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN on a pre-existing table. Relying on it to add fcc_* columns to the already-created challenges table is a silent no-op, so the actual mechanism that added those columns on the live box is unknown/unreproducible — a reproducibility violation.
findings.evidence: fcc_sync.py:264 comment claims `# Ensure all tables exist (handles new columns gracefully)`. But challenges already exists from migration 704755926c31, so create_all() skips it and the fcc_* columns are never added by this call. Yet upsert_challenge writes `existing.fcc_description = description` (fcc_sync.py:158) which would raise OperationalError: no such column unless the columns were added some other way.
findings.recommendation: Do not use create_all() for evolving an existing table. Move the additive column adds into the reconciling migration from DBN-1. Keep create_all() (if at all) only for genuinely new tables on a virgin DB, and document that it is a bootstrap convenience, not a migration substitute. After DBN-1 lands, remove the misleading comment at fcc_sync.py:264.

findings.title: image_path nullability diverges between migration (NOT NULL) and model (nullable=True) — model/DDL disagreement
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Append-only discipline + single-source-of-truth: the deployed constraint and the model must agree. The initial migration created image_path as `nullable=False`; the model now declares `nullable=True`. Because the append-only rule (correctly) forbids a destructive ALTER to drop the NOT NULL, the live column is still NOT NULL, so the model lies about the true constraint. This is why fcc_sync inserts image_path='' (empty string) instead of NULL — a workaround for a constraint the model claims does not exist.
findings.evidence: Migration: `sa.Column("image_path", sa.String(length=255), nullable=False)`. Model: `image_path = db.Column(db.String(255), nullable=True)  # Nullable: FCC-synced challenges may not have images`. fcc_sync.py:173 `image_path="",  # Empty string satisfies NOT NULL`.
findings.recommendation: Do NOT alter the live NOT NULL constraint (append-only). Instead make the model honest: revert image_path to `nullable=False` in models.py:29-31 and keep the documented empty-string sentinel convention. If a true NULL semantic is ever needed, add a new nullable column (e.g. has_image Boolean) rather than mutating the existing constraint. Add a comment cross-referencing the empty-string convention so future readers do not 'fix' the model to nullable and then attempt a destructive migration.

findings.title: concept_strengths and user_notebooks are never written by any code path — unpopulated relations with mandatory FKs
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Normalization presupposes that a relation models a real fact set with a defined insertion path. A relation with NOT NULL foreign keys (user_id, challenge_id) but zero writers is a schema whose tuples can never legitimately come into existence through the application — it is dead schema. This is not a normal-form violation per se, but it undermines the closed-world assumption relational design depends on and creates confusion about the true data model.
findings.evidence: Grep across all *.py shows ConceptStrength( / UserNotebook( are never instantiated. Only usages: `strengths = ConceptStrength.query.filter_by(user_id=user.id).all()` (app.py:546) and `notebooks = UserNotebook.query.filter_by(...)` (app.py:547) — both reads, always returning []. models.py:106 score default=100 with reward/penalty comment is unwired.
findings.recommendation: Decide per table: (a) if the gameplay/notebook feature is planned, keep the tables (they are already additive and cause no harm) but add a `# NOT YET WRITTEN — read-only placeholder` docstring note and a tracking task; (b) do NOT drop them (append-only). Prioritize wiring at least one writer (e.g., create a UserNotebook row on first comment or on challenge view) so the FK-constrained relation has a valid insertion path. Until then, guard app.py:546-547 is fine since empty lists render safely.

findings.title: users.mobile mislabeled 'PRIMARY KEY lookup standard' — it is a UNIQUE secondary attribute, not the PK
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Codd's key theory: a table has exactly one primary key; other uniquely-constraining attributes are candidate/alternate keys. Here `id` is the declared PK (models.py:77) and `mobile` is an alternate key. Calling mobile the 'PRIMARY KEY lookup standard' conflates alternate key with primary key. It is also a semantically fragile natural key: phone numbers get reassigned/reused and are formatted inconsistently (String(20), no normalization), so promoting it to any kind of identity risks update anomalies and false-duplicate/false-unique behavior.
findings.evidence: models.py:77 `id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)`; models.py:80 `mobile = db.Column(db.String(20), unique=True, nullable=False) # PRIMARY KEY lookup standard`. app.py:481 dedupes on `(User.email == email) | (User.mobile == mobile)` — treats email and mobile equally as alternate keys.
findings.recommendation: Correct the comment to reflect reality: `# alternate/candidate key (UNIQUE); surrogate id is the PK`. Keep the surrogate integer `id` as PK (correct choice). Do not change the constraint (append-only + it is a legitimate UNIQUE). Optionally add server-side normalization (strip spaces/leading +) before insert so the UNIQUE constraint compares canonical forms and does not admit '+1 555...' vs '1555...' as distinct.

findings.title: challenge_number is a redundant candidate key that is never enforced UNIQUE and never used — potential 3NF transitive-dependency / consistency gap
findings.methodologyPrinciple: 3NF + key theory: in FCC's model, date_id and challenge_number are two candidate keys that both functionally determine the same row (date <-> sequence number is a 1:1 mapping). Storing both without a UNIQUE constraint on challenge_number means the pair can drift out of sync (two rows could share a challenge_number, or a manual row could have NULL while a synced row has a value), yet nothing enforces the functional dependency date_id -> challenge_number. It is a latent update/insertion-anomaly surface.
findings.evidence: models.py:41-43 `challenge_number = db.Column(db.Integer, nullable=True)` — nullable, NOT unique. Ordering everywhere is `Challenge.date_id.desc()` (app.py:123,166,401), lexicographic on ISO YYYY-MM-DD which coincidentally matches chronological order; challenge_number is never used for ordering or lookup — only echoed in api_challenge JSON (app.py:437). Manual-created rows (app.py:368) never set challenge_number, so it stays NULL for manual challenges.
findings.recommendation: Keep challenge_number (harmless, additive) but treat date_id as the sole natural key (it already is: unique+not-null, models.py:25-27). To protect the functional dependency additively, add a UNIQUE constraint on challenge_number in the DBN-1 migration guarded to allow multiple NULLs (SQLite treats NULLs as distinct, so nullable UNIQUE is safe for manual rows). Do not switch ordering to challenge_number (date_id ordering is correct and covers manual rows that lack a number). Document that date_id is canonical and challenge_number is FCC-provenance metadata only.

findings.title: Denormalized fcc_* text blobs store multi-valued/serialized JSON in single columns — 1NF atomicity trade-off
locations: portfolio-v2/app.py:224-239 (json.loads + regex re-parse on every detail render)
findings.methodologyPrinciple: 1NF requires attribute atomicity — each cell holds a single indivisible value, not a repeating group. fcc_js_tests/fcc_py_tests hold a JSON-serialized LIST of test objects ({text, testString}), i.e. a repeating group flattened into one Text column. Strict 1NF would model tests as a child table challenge_tests(challenge_id, lang, ordinal, text, test_string).
findings.evidence: models.py:47 `fcc_js_tests = db.Column(db.Text, ...) # JSON string of JS test cases`. fcc_sync.py:141 `py_tests = json.dumps(py_data.get("tests", []), ...)`. app.py:225 `raw_py_tests = json.loads(challenge.fcc_py_tests)` then per-test regex extraction each render.
findings.recommendation: This is an acceptable pragmatic denormalization for a read-mostly, single-parent blob that is always consumed whole client-side, and normalizing to a child table would add join complexity for no query benefit (tests are never queried individually in SQL). RECOMMENDATION: keep as-is but (a) document the deliberate 1NF trade-off in the model comment, and (b) move the regex/unescape parsing (app.py:227-235) out of the request path into fcc_sync at write time — store the already-extracted pure-Python assertions in a new additive column (e.g. fcc_py_tests_parsed Text) so the detail render does not re-parse JSON+regex on every hit. This keeps the denormalization but removes repeated work.

topPriorities: DBN-1: Author a single additive, idempotent Alembic migration (down_revision=49b711d2aba8) that reconciles the deployed schema to models.py — add challenges.challenge_number/fcc_*/source via guarded batch_op.add_column and create users/comments/concept_strengths/user_notebooks via has_table-guarded create_table; downgrade = no-op (append-only). This makes migrations the single source of truth again.

DBN-2 & DBN-3: Stop relying on db.create_all() to evolve existing tables (it silently no-ops on column adds) — remove/quiet the misleading comment at fcc_sync.py:264, and make models.py honest by reverting image_path to nullable=False to match the live NOT NULL constraint and the empty-string sentinel convention.

DBN-6/DBN-5: Codify date_id as the sole canonical natural key (it already is), correct the users.mobile 'PRIMARY KEY' comment to 'alternate key', and add a nullable-safe UNIQUE constraint on challenge_number in the same reconciling migration to enforce the date_id->challenge_number functional dependency.
audits.summary: The schema is broadly reasonable (surrogate integer PKs, a clean date_id natural key, sensible FKs, foreign_keys=ON) but suffers one dominant, systemic defect: migrations are NOT the source of truth. The Alembic head (49b711d2aba8) describes a challenges table with 9 columns and no users/comments/concept_strengths/user_notebooks tables, while models.py declares 17 challenge columns plus 4 additional tables. The gap is bridged only by out-of-band db.create_all() calls in fcc_sync.py and the create_*_tables.py scripts — and critically, create_all() cannot add columns to an already-existing table, so the mechanism that actually added the fcc_* columns on the live box is unreproducible (DBN-1, DBN-2). This violates append-only migration discipline, which requires the migration chain to be a complete, replayable source of truth. Secondary issues: image_path nullability disagrees between migration (NOT NULL) and model (nullable=True), which the code silently works around with empty-string sentinels (DBN-3); concept_strengths and user_notebooks are FK-constrained tables with zero writers (dead-but-additive schema, DBN-4); users.mobile is mislabeled a 'PRIMARY KEY' when it is an alternate key on a fragile natural attribute (DBN-5); challenge_number is a redundant candidate key that is nullable, non-unique, and never used for identity or ordering, leaving the date_id->challenge_number functional dependency unenforced (DBN-6, borderline 3NF); and fcc_*_tests store JSON repeating-groups in single columns, a deliberate 1NF trade-off that additionally forces per-request re-parsing (DBN-7). The fix strategy is uniform and honors the HARD append-only rule: never DROP; instead author ONE additive, idempotent reconciling migration (guarded add_column / create_table, no-op downgrade), make the model honest about the live NOT NULL constraint, and retire create_all() as a schema-evolution mechanism. Server-only items (DBN-1, DBN-2, DBN-6) require running `flask db upgrade` against the live SQLite file on 192.168.1.150 and are deferred to production; model-comment and code-path fixes (DBN-3, DBN-4, DBN-5, DBN-7) can be made now in the repo.

audits.methodology: Single Source of Truth (SSoT) + referential integrity + data-lineage mapping. Grounding principles: (P1) every logical datum has exactly one canonical owner/parent; (P2) all other representations are derived/referenced from that parent (via FK or real-time derivation), never independently stored copies that can drift; (P3) referential integrity — a reference (image_path, FK) must always resolve to an existing, unique target; (P4) lineage traceability — you can trace each rendered value back to its single origin; (P5) additive/forward-compatible migration only (project hard rule). The newly added GET /api/challenge/<date_id> is the reference implementation of P2/P4 (calendar derives content live from the one Challenge row).

findings.title: Same challenge image duplicated across two directories with three naming schemes — only one is referenced by the DB, the rest are orphans that can drift
findings.evidence: Filesystem listing shows the 2026-01-31 zodiac challenge exists as THREE files: `static/daily_challenges/2026-01-31_zodiac-sign.jpg` (dash-format, wrong dir), `static/images/20260131_zodiac_sign.jpg`, and `static/images/20260131_challenge_2026_01_31.jpg`. The DB stores exactly one `image_path` string; every app path (app.py:39 UPLOAD_FOLDER, image_processor.py:15 IMAGE_DIR, telegram_bridge.py:270) reads/writes only `static/images/`. `static/daily_challenges/` is referenced by NO code path.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P1 (single canonical owner) and P3 (referential integrity): the challenge image is a single logical datum but exists as multiple physical copies; the DB reference resolves to one, leaving 1-2 orphan copies with no owner. If the wrong one is edited (e.g. re-cropped by image_processor.py which overwrites in place), the rendered thumbnail silently drifts from the intended source.
findings.recommendation: Declare `static/images/<YYYYMMDD>_<slug>.jpg` the sole canonical image store. (1) Delete the stray `static/daily_challenges/` directory after confirming its file is superseded. (2) Write a one-off reconciliation script that, for each Challenge row, verifies `os.path.exists(join(UPLOAD_FOLDER, image_path))` and reports/deletes any `static/images/*.jpg` file not referenced by any row's image_path (orphan sweep). (3) Enforce a single deterministic filename per date_id at write time so a second upload for the same date overwrites rather than creating a new-named twin (see SSOT-2).

findings.title: Admin re-upload for the same date creates a new differently-named file, orphaning the old image the DB previously pointed to
findings.evidence: `_save_uploaded_file` builds `filename = f"{date_str.replace('-','')}_{prefix}_{safe_title}.{ext}"` (app.py:343). The filename embeds `safe_title`, so re-uploading the same date with a corrected title yields a NEW filename; `_handle_admin_post` sets `challenge.image_path = main_image` (app.py:372) to the new name and commits, but the previously-referenced file is left on disk forever. The Telegram path is worse: `final_filename = f"{target_date.replace('-','')}_{msg_id}_sync.jpg"` (telegram_bridge.py:286) embeds the Telegram `msg_id`, so every re-send of the same day's photo produces a uniquely-named file and a new orphan, while image_path flips to the latest.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P1/P3: date_id is the natural key (unique) for a day's challenge, so its image should have one deterministic canonical filename derived from that key. Deriving the filename from mutable/incidental data (title, msg_id) breaks the 1:1 datum↔file invariant and accumulates orphans that can be mistakenly served or re-processed.
findings.recommendation: Make the filename a pure function of the canonical key only: `f"{date_str.replace('-','')}.{ext}"` (or `{date}_{prefix}.{ext}` for the fixed set of screenshot roles). Because the name is deterministic, a re-upload overwrites the same file — image_path never needs to change and no orphan is produced. If a title-based slug is desired for aesthetics, store the slug in a separate column and keep the on-disk name key-derived.

findings.title: Daily motivational quote is persisted in two independent stores (quote_cache.json vs Challenge.quote_text) with no lineage between them
findings.evidence: Two quote lineages exist. (A) `get_daily_quote()` maintains an hourly file cache at `data/quote_cache.json` and home() renders `live_quote` from it (app.py:142,157). (B) The admin form writes a per-challenge quote into `challenge.quote_text` (app.py:376) — sourced from `get_fcc_quote()` if blank (app.py:355-356) — and migration `f8faeae5ecde` added the `quote_text` column for it. These two never reconcile: `quote_text` is written but NEVER read by any template or route (grep shows no `.quote_text` read; home.html renders only `live_quote`). So the DB column is write-only dead data, and the displayed quote comes from a JSON file that no other feature can see.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P1/P4: the 'quote shown for a challenge' is one logical datum but has two disconnected owners (a JSON cache and a DB column) and the rendered value's lineage traces to neither consistently. quote_text is stored but unreferenced (broken lineage — a stored value no consumer derives from).
findings.recommendation: Pick one owner. Option A (simplest): treat the site-wide rotating quote as ephemeral display state — keep quote_cache.json as the canonical (it is already the only thing rendered) and DROP writing quote_text going forward (leave the nullable column per append-only rule, but stop populating it, or document it as deprecated). Option B (if a per-challenge quote is a real feature): make challenge_detail render `challenge.quote_text` and stop using the global cache on that page — then quote_text becomes the single source for that view and the file cache is only the homepage rotator. Decide which view owns which quote and remove the dual-write.

findings.title: Homepage stats are fabricated constants, not derived from the data they claim to summarize
findings.evidence: `total_challenges = Challenge.query.count()` then `mastered = total_challenges` (app.py:128) and `in_progress = 1` (app.py:129), and `progress_percent = round((mastered/total_challenges)*100,1)` which is therefore ALWAYS 100.0. There is no per-challenge status field in the model, yet the UI presents 'mastered', 'in_progress', and a progress percentage as if computed from real state.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P2/P4 (derivation + traceability): a computed/aggregate value must be derived from its underlying source rows. Here the aggregates are hard-coded (in_progress=1, mastered=total) so they cannot be traced to any per-challenge truth — they will misreport the moment the intended semantics (some challenges not yet solved) exist.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) remove the mastered/in_progress/progress fields until a real per-challenge status exists, showing only the honest `total = Challenge.query.count()`; or (b) if progress is a genuine feature, add a nullable `status` column (append-only compliant) and compute `mastered = Challenge.query.filter_by(status='mastered').count()`, `in_progress = ...`, deriving progress_percent from those counts. Do not ship constants dressed as computed stats.

findings.title: Model/migration schema drift: image_path is NOT NULL in the migration/live DB but declared nullable in the model, forcing a sentinel empty-string convention
findings.evidence: The initial migration created `sa.Column("image_path", sa.String(255), nullable=False)` (migration line 26). The current model declares `image_path = db.Column(db.String(255), nullable=True)` (models.py:30). The live SQLite schema follows the migration (NOT NULL) because SQLite can't drop the constraint without a destructive migration. Consequently fcc_sync.py inserts `image_path=""` (empty string sentinel) rather than NULL (fcc_sync.py:173, comment lines 168-169), and every template checks truthiness of image_path instead of `is not None`.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P4 (lineage/traceability) + P5 (additive migration): the schema definition (single source describing the column's contract) disagrees between model and DB. The 'canonical schema' is ambiguous — is empty-string a valid image or an absent one? Two conventions (empty-string vs NULL for 'no image') now both mean 'absent', which is a semantic-duplication drift.
findings.recommendation: Standardize on ONE 'no image' representation. Keep empty-string as the canonical sentinel (least churn, already used everywhere): (1) update models.py:30 back to `nullable=False, default=""` so the model matches the live DB contract and the empty-string convention is explicit and enforced; (2) add a comment/constant `NO_IMAGE = ""` used by fcc_sync.py and any writer; (3) ensure all readers use `if challenge.image_path:` (truthiness) uniformly — the new /api/challenge already does via `bool(c.image_path)` (app.py:420). This is additive-safe (no drop).

findings.title: Dead duplicate lineage: load_challenges() re-derives challenges from filenames, a parallel source of truth that competes with the DB
findings.evidence: `load_challenges()` scans `static/images/*.jpg`, parses `date_str = filename[:8]` and `name = filename[9:-4].replace('_',' ').title()` (app.py:101-102) to synthesize challenge dicts with date/name/image. This is a second, filesystem-derived source of challenge truth that parallels the DB. Grep confirms it is defined but NEVER called (only the def appears). Critically, its parser assumes `YYYYMMDD_name.jpg`, which does NOT match the admin naming `YYYYMMDD_<prefix>_<title>.jpg` (app.py:343) or the Telegram `YYYYMMDD_<msgid>_sync.jpg` (telegram_bridge.py:286) — so if ever re-enabled it would mislabel every current file.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P1 (single owner): the DB Challenge table is the declared owner of challenge metadata; load_challenges() reconstructs the same logical data from filenames, creating a latent competing source that is already inconsistent with the real naming scheme. Dead code that encodes a stale second lineage is a drift hazard.
findings.recommendation: Delete `load_challenges()` (app.py:85-114) entirely — the DB is the single source and this function is unreferenced and would produce wrong data if revived. Removing it eliminates the filename-as-truth alternative and the stale parser assumption.

findings.title: image_processor.clean_image overwrites files in place (non-idempotent), so the on-disk image can silently diverge from the ingested original
findings.evidence: `clean_image()` does `cv2.imwrite(image_path, final_img)` (line 48), overwriting the source. `main()` globs `static/images/*.jpg` and processes everything (lines 52-57) with the comment 'Or just process everything, it's fine for small sets' — and the Telegram '🧼 Clean' button runs image_processor.py against the whole directory (telegram_bridge.py:138). Because cropping is relative (top 12% / below 94% of current height, lines 26-27), re-running re-crops an already-cropped image, progressively destroying it. There is no untouched original retained.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P4 (lineage traceability): a derived artifact (cleaned/framed thumbnail) should be reproducible from a retained canonical original. Overwriting in place destroys the source, so the lineage from original→displayed image is unrecoverable and each re-clean drifts the artifact further from any ground truth.
findings.recommendation: Keep the raw upload as the canonical original and write the cleaned version to a derived name/location (e.g. store originals in `static/images/_raw/<name>.jpg` and cleaned output as `static/images/<name>.jpg`), OR add an idempotency guard (embed a marker/EXIF flag or skip files whose dimensions already match the cropped ratio) so re-runs are no-ops. This makes clean_image a pure, repeatable derivation from a preserved source.

findings.title: Bot config (grok_key) and admin identity (admin_id.txt) live only in server-side flat files, disconnected from the DB and from each other's lifecycle
findings.evidence: `claw_config.json` holds `{grok_key}` and `admin_id.txt` holds the admin chat id; both are read via bespoke file loaders (telegram_bridge.py:33-46). The admin id is auto-claimed to whoever messages first (lines 60-63, 87-90). These are authoritative runtime state but have no representation in portfolio.db and no relationship to the User table (which has its own claude_token/github_token columns, models.py:86-87). There are thus two disjoint notions of 'the owner/credentials' with no shared source of truth.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: P1 (single owner of a datum): 'who is the admin' and 'which API keys the system uses' are each a single logical datum but are stored in ad-hoc files outside the system's canonical datastore, unlinked to the User model that already exists for identity/credentials. This fragments the identity/credential lineage across files + DB with no reconciliation.
findings.recommendation: This is a design/consolidation note (server-only state). Longer term, promote admin identity and service credentials into a single canonical store — either a dedicated `settings`/`app_config` table (additive) or a documented server secrets file that is the ONE source, and have get_admin()/load_config() read from it. At minimum, document that admin_id.txt + claw_config.json are the canonical server state and are intentionally outside the DB, so the split is deliberate rather than accidental drift.

topPriorities: SSOT-1: Eliminate duplicate challenge images — delete the stray static/daily_challenges/ dir and the orphaned 20260131_* twin, then run an orphan-sweep so every static/images file is referenced by exactly one Challenge.image_path (P1/P3).

SSOT-2: Make the on-disk image filename a deterministic function of date_id only (drop title/msg_id from the name) so re-uploads overwrite instead of spawning new orphans — this is the root cause that produced SSOT-1.
SSOT-3: Resolve the dual quote lineage (data/quote_cache.json vs write-only Challenge.quote_text): pick one owner per view and stop the dual-write; quote_text is currently stored but never read.
SSOT-4: Stop presenting fabricated constants (mastered=total, in_progress=1, progress=100%) as computed stats — either derive from a real status column or remove them (P2/P4).
SSOT-5: Fix model/migration drift on image_path (model says nullable, DB says NOT NULL) and codify empty-string as the single canonical 'no image' sentinel used by all readers.
audits.summary: Measured against Single-Source-of-Truth + referential-integrity + lineage principles, the challenge row in portfolio.db is a well-intentioned canonical parent (and the new GET /api/challenge/<date_id> is an exemplary real-time-derivation consumer that eliminates page-embedded duplication), but several data are still stored in two places or derived incorrectly. The most concrete drift is on the FILESYSTEM side of the image lineage: the 2026-01-31 challenge physically exists as three files across two directories (static/daily_challenges/ + two differently-named twins in static/images/), while the DB references only one — the rest are ownerless orphans. The root cause (SSOT-2) is that both the admin uploader and the Telegram pipeline derive filenames from mutable/incidental data (title, Telegram msg_id) instead of the canonical date_id key, so any re-upload spawns a new-named twin and orphans the previous file; image_processor further destroys lineage by overwriting originals in place (SSOT-7). On the DB side, three write-but-never-read or fabricated data violate derivation/traceability: quote_text is dual-stored against quote_cache.json and never rendered (SSOT-3), homepage mastered/in_progress/progress are hard-coded constants masquerading as aggregates (SSOT-4), and image_path suffers a model(nullable)-vs-migration(NOT NULL) schema drift papered over with an empty-string sentinel (SSOT-5). Two latent hazards round it out: a dead load_challenges() that re-derives challenge truth from filenames with a now-wrong parser (SSOT-6), and admin/credential state fragmented across claw_config.json + admin_id.txt with no link to the User table (SSOT-8). Fixes are almost entirely additive and low-risk; the priority chain is SSOT-2 (deterministic key-derived filenames) → SSOT-1 (orphan cleanup) → SSOT-3/4/5 (retire duplicate/fabricated DB data), which collectively restore a single traceable lineage per datum without any destructive migration.

audits.methodology: Observability (structured logging / metrics / health-and-readiness endpoints) + Graceful Degradation + Circuit-Breaker / Retry-with-Backoff patterns. Reference framework: Google SRE Book ("Monitoring Distributed Systems", the four golden signals, and "Handling Overload"), the Twelve-Factor App factor XI ("Treat logs as event streams"), and the Nygard/Fowler resilience-patterns canon (Release It! — Circuit Breaker, Retry with exponential backoff+jitter, Fail Fast, Fail Safe/Graceful Degradation, Health-Check API). Core principle applied throughout: a self-healing system must (1) make failure OBSERVABLE, (2) DEGRADE gracefully with fallbacks that SURFACE rather than HIDE the failure, and (3) RETRY transient faults with bounded backoff while a supervisor restarts only on a verified-unhealthy signal.

findings.title: No /health or /readiness endpoint — watchdog and tunnel restart blindly on a network ping, never on app health
locations: portfolio-v2/app.py:117-159 (only routes; no health route anywhere in the 653-line file)
findings.evidence: app.py has no route matching health/readiness/healthz (grep for 'health|readiness|healthz' in *.py returns only telegram_bridge.py's 'bot_health.log' filename). The watchdog's only health signal is `ping -c 1 -W 3 8.8.8.8` (network_watchdog.sh:25) and `systemctl is-active --quiet portfolio` (line 58). A gunicorn worker that is deadlocked on a wedged SQLite write (WAL lock), stuck on the 15s FCC HTTP call, or serving 500s will still be `active` to systemd and will still pass the internet ping, so no recovery ever fires.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Observability — Health-Check API (Release It!) / SRE readiness-vs-liveness distinction. A supervisor must restart on a *verified-unhealthy* signal, not a proxy signal (process-alive or internet-up). Restarting on the wrong signal is the definition of 'restarts blindly'.
findings.recommendation: Add a liveness route `GET /health` returning `200 {"status":"ok"}` cheaply (no DB), and a readiness route `GET /readiness` that runs `db.session.execute(text('SELECT 1'))` inside try/except and returns `200 {"db":"ok"}` or `503 {"db":"down","error":...}`. Then change network_watchdog.sh line 58 from `systemctl is-active` to `curl -fsS --max-time 5 http://localhost:5001/readiness` and only restart on non-2xx (retry the curl 2-3x before acting to avoid flapping). This makes recovery driven by real app health.

findings.title: print() used as the app's logging channel — no structured logging, levels, or log stream in the Flask app
findings.evidence: Every error path in app.py surfaces via bare `print(...)` — e.g. `except Exception as e: print(f"Error loading daily challenge: {e}")` (line 124-125). There is no `logging.getLogger`, no level (INFO/WARN/ERROR), no timestamp, no request context, and no stack trace. Under gunicorn these prints land in stdout with no severity, un-parseable and un-filterable. Contrast telegram_bridge.py:24 which *does* configure `logging.basicConfig` — the web app is the inconsistent one.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Observability + Twelve-Factor XI ('treat logs as event streams'). Logs must be leveled, timestamped, and machine-parseable so failures are detectable/alertable. print() discards severity and context, so failures cannot be surfaced to monitoring.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a module-level `logger = logging.getLogger('portfolio')` with `logging.basicConfig(level=INFO, format='%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s %(message)s')` (mirror telegram_bridge.py:24). Replace each print with `logger.error("loading latest challenge failed", exc_info=True)` (exc_info gives the traceback). For production, wire a RotatingFileHandler to data/app.log and/or let gunicorn capture stderr. Consider JSON logs (python-json-logger) so lines are queryable.

findings.title: No retry/backoff or circuit-breaker on any outbound FCC / GitHub-raw / Telegram / LLM HTTP call — single timeout = silent skip
findings.evidence: fetch_challenge does `resp = requests.get(url, timeout=15)` once (fcc_sync.py:68); on `requests.exceptions.RequestException` it logs and `return None` (line 77-79). A single transient blip (DNS, TLS reset, 502) permanently drops that day's challenge until the *next* cron run 24h later. backfill() (fcc_sync.py:220-244) just tallies it as an `error` and moves on. No `urllib3.util.Retry`, no `HTTPAdapter(max_retries=...)`, no exponential backoff, no circuit breaker anywhere in the codebase.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Retry-with-exponential-backoff+jitter (Release It! / AWS Builders' Library) and Circuit Breaker. Transient network faults are the expected failure mode for a home-internet-hosted box (the watchdog exists precisely because outages are expected), yet the fetch path makes exactly one attempt and gives up.
findings.recommendation: Create a shared `requests.Session` with `HTTPAdapter(max_retries=urllib3.util.Retry(total=3, backoff_factor=0.5, status_forcelist=[429,500,502,503,504], respect_retry_after_header=True))` and reuse it for FCC and quote fetches. For the daily sync, if all retries fail, escalate to WARN-level log AND record a persisted 'sync-failed' marker (see SH-8) so the failure surfaces rather than silently waiting a day. For /api/rate and LLM calls, wrap in a simple circuit breaker (e.g. `pybreaker`) so a dead Telegram/xAI endpoint doesn't add latency to every request.

findings.title: Silent-swallow error handling (bare try/except pass) hides failures instead of surfacing them
locations: portfolio-v2/app.py:640-643 (/api/rate: `except Exception as e: pass # Fault tolerance`)
portfolio-v2/app.py:297-298 (quote cache read: `except Exception: pass  # Cache corrupt`)
portfolio-v2/fcc_sync.py:49-54 (log(): `except Exception: pass` — logging failures are themselves swallowed)
portfolio-v2/app.py:454-459 (/read/<token>: `except Exception: return ...404` — masks template errors as 404)
findings.evidence: /api/rate: `try: requests.get(...telegram...) except Exception as e: pass # Fault tolerance` then unconditionally `return jsonify({"success": True, "message": "Log Pushed!"})` (app.py:640-645). The endpoint reports success to the client even when the notification was never delivered — the `e` is bound but discarded. Likewise the quote cache read (app.py:297) and even the log() helper (fcc_sync.py:53) swallow all exceptions with `pass`, so a broken log directory silently blackholes every diagnostic line.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Graceful Degradation must SURFACE, not HIDE, failures (Fail-Safe ≠ Fail-Silent). Swallowing exceptions with bare `pass` and returning `success:True` violates the observability principle that every failure must leave a trace; it converts a recoverable/alertable fault into an invisible one.
findings.recommendation: Every `except: pass` must at minimum `logger.warning(...)` (or `exc_info=True` for unexpected ones) so the failure is observable. /api/rate should return a truthful status: `{"success": false, "notified": false}` (or 502) when the Telegram call fails, so the client/monitoring knows delivery failed. Narrow the /read handler to `except TemplateNotFound` so real render errors (which should be 500 + logged) are not masked as 404.

findings.title: home() computes stats with an UNGUARDED DB query after the guarded one — a DB fault yields a 500 with no fallback
findings.evidence: The latest-challenge query IS wrapped: `try: challenge = Challenge.query...first() except Exception as e: print(...)` (lines 122-125). But immediately after, `total_challenges = Challenge.query.count()` (line 127) runs OUTSIDE any try/except, and the stats dict divides `(mastered / total_challenges)` (line 136). If the DB is locked/unavailable, the first query is caught but the second raises unhandled → the entire home page 500s. The partial guard gives a false sense of resilience.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Graceful Degradation — the homepage is the highest-traffic path and should degrade to a usable page (empty stats) rather than a hard 500 when the DB is transiently unavailable. Inconsistent guarding (one query protected, the next not) is a latent fail-open/fail-hard mismatch.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the full data-fetch block (latest challenge + count) in one try/except that logs and falls back to `challenge=None, stats={'total':0,...}`, rendering a degraded but functional page. Better: add an errorhandler(500) and errorhandler(404) that render a branded page and `logger.error(exc_info=True)`, so no unhandled route ever leaks a raw stack trace.

findings.title: Watchdog recovery has no verification, no logging of sync exit code, and can loop-restart via unconditional sudo
locations: portfolio-v2/network_watchdog.sh:48-49 (recovery only fires on offline→online edge)
findings.evidence: On recovery the watchdog runs `$VENV_PYTHON "$SYNC_SCRIPT" >> "$LOG_FILE" 2>&1` (line 55) but never checks `$?` — a crashed sync is indistinguishable from success in the state machine. It then `sudo systemctl restart portfolio` (line 60) / nginx (line 67) but never re-checks that the service came *back* healthy afterward (no post-restart `is-active` re-check, no /readiness probe). Recovery actions are also gated purely on the `offline→online` edge (line 49): if the app is wedged while the internet stays up, the watchdog never intervenes at all (ties to SH-1).
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Self-healing loops must CLOSE the loop: act → verify → escalate. This restarts without confirming the fault cleared (no verification) and swallows the sync exit status (no observability of whether catch-up succeeded).
findings.recommendation: Capture `$VENV_PYTHON $SYNC_SCRIPT; rc=$?; log "catch-up sync exited rc=$rc"`. After each `systemctl restart`, re-probe (`sleep 3; systemctl is-active --quiet portfolio || log 'RESTART FAILED — escalating'`) and, ideally, curl /readiness (SH-1). Decouple health-recovery from the network edge: on every tick, if /readiness is unhealthy, attempt one restart with a cooldown counter in the state file to prevent restart storms (fail-fast + backoff on the restart itself).

findings.title: db.create_all() as implicit schema self-heal masks migration drift with no observability
findings.evidence: fcc_sync.py runs `db.create_all()` on every invocation (line 265) with the comment `# Ensure all tables exist (handles new columns gracefully)`. Per 03-data-model.md the fcc_* columns and user/comment tables exist ONLY via create_all(), not migrations. create_all() creates missing *tables* but silently does NOT add missing *columns* to an existing table — so a partially-migrated DB will not self-heal and will instead throw OperationalError('no such column') at query time on the web path, with no startup check to surface it.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Fail-Fast on startup for unrecoverable config/schema problems, and observability of self-heal actions. An implicit repair that can only half-work, runs silently, and defers the failure to a user request is the opposite of a surfaced, verifiable heal.
findings.recommendation: Add a startup readiness check (reuse SH-1's /readiness or an app-factory check) that runs a canary `SELECT` touching the fcc_* columns and logs.error + refuses ready if a column is missing, pointing the operator to run migrations. Prefer `flask db upgrade` (Alembic) as the single source of schema truth over scattered create_all() scripts; if create_all() is kept, log at INFO exactly which tables it created so the heal is observable.

findings.title: Daily sync failure is invisible — no success/failure metric, freshness marker, or alert
findings.evidence: sync_today() logs `No data returned...` to a text file on failure (fcc_sync.py:201-202) and exits 0 regardless. The cron line just redirects to cron_sync.log (setup_server.sh:59). There is no emitted metric, no 'last successful sync' timestamp file, and no alert (the Telegram bot could notify but does not). Because the fetch also has no retry (SH-3), a single 00:30 blip means the day silently has no challenge and nobody is informed until a human notices the site.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Observability — the SRE 'freshness' signal for a data pipeline. A batch job must emit a heartbeat/last-success timestamp so staleness is detectable; a job that fails silently and exits 0 cannot be monitored.
findings.recommendation: On successful upsert, write `data/last_sync_ok.json` = `{ts, date_id, action}`; expose its age in /readiness (warn if > 26h old). On failure after retries, send a Telegram alert via the existing bot token/chat and log.error. Make cron exit non-zero on failure so a systemd OnFailure= handler (or `MAILTO`) can fire. This turns a silent skip into a surfaced, actionable signal.

findings.title: API endpoints return unstructured/untyped errors and lie about outcomes; no consistent JSON error envelope
locations: portfolio-v2/app.py:398-407 (/api/challenges: no try/except; DB error → HTML 500)
portfolio-v2/app.py:416-418 (/api/challenge/<id>: 404 body ok, but no 5xx handling)
findings.evidence: /api/rate hardcodes `return jsonify({"success": True, "message": "Log Pushed!"})` even when the Telegram GET raised (app.py:645, see SH-4). /api/challenges (app.py:401) runs a raw query with no error handling — a DB fault returns Flask's default HTML 500 to a JSON client, which cannot parse it. There is no app-wide JSON error handler, so API consumers get inconsistent content-types on failure.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Graceful Degradation + observability at the API contract layer: clients (and the calendar modal that now depends on /api/challenge per the recent fix) must receive typed, truthful error responses to react correctly rather than a success lie or an unparseable HTML page.
findings.recommendation: Register `@app.errorhandler(Exception)` (and 404/500) that returns `jsonify({'error': type(e).__name__, 'message': str(e)}), 500` for /api/* paths (branch on request.path or use a Blueprint with its own handler), and log.error(exc_info=True). Fix /api/rate to report `notified: bool`. Standardize a `{ok, data|error}` envelope across the JSON API.

findings.title: get_public_url.sh uses fixed sleeps instead of readiness polling and `set -e` can abort the fallback chain
findings.evidence: Each method waits a hard `sleep 8` (TIMEOUT=8) then greps once for the URL (lines 32-33). A tunnel that needs 9s is declared failed; one ready in 2s wastes 6s. `set -e` at line 7 means any unguarded non-zero (e.g., wget/unzip in try_ngrok:46-48) can abort the whole script before the localhost.run/serveo fallbacks are tried, defeating the 4-method resilience design.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Retry patterns should poll for readiness with a deadline, not sleep-and-hope; and a fail-fast (`set -e`) global setting must not short-circuit an intentional graceful-degradation fallback chain.
findings.recommendation: Replace fixed sleeps with a poll loop: `for i in $(seq 1 15); do URL=$(extract_url $LOG); [ -n "$URL" ] && break; sleep 1; done`. Guard the fallback functions so a single method's failure never aborts the chain (they already `return 1`, but ensure `set -e` doesn't trip inside them — wrap risky commands with `|| true`, as kill_all already does). Log which method succeeded for observability.

findings.title: Telegram bot LLM/shell retry logic has no backoff and swallows the ReAct loop-exhaustion case as a generic message
findings.evidence: The Gemini model fallback loop (telegram_bridge.py:177-201) tries 4 models sequentially with no backoff and concatenates errors into a user-facing string. If the ReAct loop hits max_loops=3 without a final answer, `final_reply` stays empty and is replaced by a hardcoded 'AI Empty Response Error' message (line 240-241) that misattributes loop-exhaustion to a 'safety block'. The outer `except Exception as e` (line 245) collapses all failure modes (network, auth, rate-limit) into one opaque '❌ AI Error' with no level/metric.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Observability + retry hygiene: distinct failure modes (rate-limit vs auth vs loop-exhaustion) must be distinguishable in logs and ideally retried with backoff on transient (429/503) responses, not merged into one misleading message.
findings.recommendation: Add exponential backoff on 429/503 from the LLM providers (respect Retry-After). Distinguish loop-exhaustion (log.warning 'ReAct max_loops reached') from an actually-empty model reply. Ensure the outer except does `logger.error(exc_info=True)` (it currently only edits the Telegram message). Note: this is provider-specific to xAI Grok / Google Gemini — the app does not use Anthropic — so no Claude-specific handling applies.

topPriorities: SH-1: Add GET /health (liveness) and GET /readiness (DB SELECT 1) endpoints, then repoint network_watchdog.sh (line 58) and monitoring at /readiness so recovery triggers on real app health, not on a 8.8.8.8 ping or bare process-liveness. This is the linchpin — SH-6, SH-7, and SH-8 all plug into it.

SH-3: Add retry-with-backoff (urllib3 Retry / HTTPAdapter) to the FCC fetch in fcc_sync.py:66-79 and the quote fetches (app.py:267,305) so a single transient blip at 00:30 cron does not silently drop the day's challenge for 24h.

SH-2 + SH-4: Replace all print()-based error reporting (app.py:125,277,314,326) with a leveled logger, and stop swallowing failures — /api/rate (app.py:640-645) must not return success:True when the Telegram notify raised, and every `except: pass` must log so failures become observable.

SH-5: Wrap home()'s full DB-fetch block (app.py:122-138) — the unguarded Challenge.query.count() and division make the homepage 500 on a transient DB lock despite the adjacent try/except; add app-wide errorhandler(500/404).
SH-8: Emit a last-successful-sync timestamp + Telegram alert on sync failure so the autonomous daily pipeline's silent failures become detectable (surface, don't hide).
audits.summary: Audited against Observability + Graceful-Degradation + Circuit-Breaker/Retry (SRE Book golden signals, Twelve-Factor XI, Nygard 'Release It!'). The system is engineered for uptime (systemd Restart=always, a 2-min network watchdog, WAL/busy-timeout SQLite hardening, a 4-method tunnel fallback) but is NOT engineered for self-healing in the methodology's sense: it lacks the three required capabilities. (1) OBSERVABILITY: there is no /health or /readiness endpoint anywhere in app.py (grep confirms the only 'health' string is the bot's log filename), and the web app reports every error via bare print() (app.py:125,277,314,326) with no levels, timestamps, or tracebacks — the failure is invisible to any monitor. (2) GRACEFUL DEGRADATION that SURFACES failure: instead, failures are HIDDEN — /api/rate swallows the Telegram error with `except Exception: pass # Fault tolerance` and still returns success:True (app.py:640-645); the quote cache and even fcc_sync's own log() helper swallow all exceptions; home() partially guards one query but leaves Challenge.query.count() unguarded so a DB blip hard-500s the front page (app.py:122-138). (3) RETRY/CIRCUIT-BREAKER: absent everywhere — fetch_challenge makes exactly one requests.get and returns None on any RequestException (fcc_sync.py:66-79), so a single 00:30 blip silently drops the day's challenge for 24h with no alert. The watchdog compounds this: it restarts on the wrong signals (internet ping + process-liveness, network_watchdog.sh:25,58), never checks the sync exit code, never re-verifies health after a restart, and never intervenes on a wedged-but-alive app. The strongest, lowest-risk lever is a real readiness endpoint that the watchdog and cron pipeline both consume; combined with a shared retry Session, a leveled logger, and truthful typed API errors, these five changes convert the project from 'blindly restarting' to genuinely self-healing. Almost all fixes are small/safe and client-reachable in local dev; the watchdog, cron-alerting, and schema-drift items (SH-6/7/8/10/11) are server-only/deferred to the 192.168.1.150 host.

audits.methodology: 12-Factor App (Factor III: Config in the environment; Factor V: Build/release/run separation; Factor X: dev/prod parity) + Principle of Least Privilege (POLP) + OWASP ASVS v4.0.3 design-level controls (V1 Architecture, V2/V4 Access Control, V6 Cryptography/secrets, V7 Error Handling & Logging, V10 Malicious Code, V14 Configuration).

findings.title: SECRET_KEY has an insecure hardcoded fallback; app boots without failing closed
findings.evidence: app.config["SECRET_KEY"] = os.environ.get("SECRET_KEY", "default-dev-key-change-this-in-env"). No .env or .env.example exists in the repo (ls .env -> No such file; git ls-files .env -> empty).
findings.methodologyPrinciple: 12-Factor Factor III (config strictly from the environment, no config in code) and OWASP ASVS V6.4/V14.2 (secrets must not be embedded; app must fail securely). A predictable fallback key means it is effectively a committed secret.
findings.recommendation: Fail closed: replace the fallback with `key = os.environ["SECRET_KEY"]` inside a try/except that sys.exit(1) with a clear message if unset (or `if not os.environ.get("SECRET_KEY") and not app.debug: raise RuntimeError(...)`). Ship a `.env.example` documenting `SECRET_KEY=` (empty) and generate the real value on the server with `python -c 'import secrets;print(secrets.token_hex(32))' >> .env`. Because auto_deploy.sh never touches .env (git checkout preserves untracked files), a server-side .env is the correct single source.

findings.title: debug=True is the only defined run path; no gunicorn invocation and no portfolio.service unit exist in the repo
findings.evidence: app.py entrypoint: `app.run(debug=True, host="127.0.0.1", port=5001)`. gunicorn is in requirements.txt but `grep -rn gunicorn *.sh *.py` returns no ExecStart/invocation. auto_deploy.sh fallback launches the debug app: `pkill -f "python app.py" ... nohup python app.py`. setup_server.sh references a `portfolio` service via drop-in override but never creates the unit's ExecStart.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: 12-Factor Factor V (strict build/release/run; runtime must not be the dev server) and Factor X (dev/prod parity done right = same server, not Werkzeug in prod). OWASP ASVS V14.1.1/V7.4.1: the Werkzeug interactive debugger permits RCE via the console PIN and leaks stack traces. Running the dev server in prod violates least-privilege of the error surface.
findings.recommendation: 1) Guard the entrypoint: `app.run(debug=os.environ.get("FLASK_DEBUG")=="1", host="127.0.0.1", port=5001)` defaulting to False. 2) Author and commit `deploy/portfolio.service` with `ExecStart=/home/nishan/portfolio/venv/bin/gunicorn -w 2 -b 127.0.0.1:5001 app:app`, `Restart=always`. 3) In auto_deploy.sh remove the `nohup python app.py` fallback and instead fail loudly if the unit is missing (the fallback silently reintroduces debug mode). Point setup_server.sh's resilience drop-in at this committed unit.

findings.title: Scoped deploy-sudo rule is defeated by fix_sudo.py's NOPASSWD:ALL and is not arg-locked
findings.evidence: setup_auto_deploy.sh installs `/etc/sudoers.d/91-portfolio-deploy` = `nishan ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /bin/systemctl restart portfolio, /usr/bin/systemctl restart portfolio`. But fix_sudo.py writes `/etc/sudoers.d/90-nishan-overrides` = `nishan ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL`. sudo evaluates all files in sudoers.d and the LAST matching rule wins; the blanket rule makes the scoping cosmetic. Also the scoped rule allows the exact command but `systemctl restart portfolio` could be satisfied by a `portfolio` unit the attacker controls if they can write unit files.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Principle of Least Privilege and OWASP ASVS V1.4.4/V4: privilege must be minimal and not silently overridable. A least-privilege rule is meaningless while a coexisting NOPASSWD:ALL rule grants root.
findings.recommendation: Before production cutover: (a) delete /etc/sudoers.d/90-nishan-overrides and retire fix_sudo.py / server_maintenance.py's sudoers writes entirely; (b) keep ONLY the scoped 91-portfolio-deploy rule; (c) pin the absolute binary path and no wildcards (already done) and remove the duplicate `/usr/bin` variant by resolving `command -v systemctl` at setup time so only one real path is authorized; (d) run `visudo -cf` validation after writing. Verify with `sudo -l` that only the restart command is permitted.

findings.title: auto_deploy.sh trusts origin/main without commit signature/integrity verification
findings.evidence: git fetch --quiet origin main; REMOTE=$(git rev-parse origin/main); git merge --ff-only origin/$BRANCH. Any commit that lands on GitHub main (compromised GitHub creds, malicious PR merge, force-push acceptance) is auto-pulled and executed within ~60s, then `python app.py`/gunicorn restarts running the new code. The repo is public and the account holds hardcoded secrets (S-1/S-2 in 08-security.md), so account takeover is plausible.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: OWASP ASVS V10.3.2 (verify application integrity / code from trusted source) and V1.14 supply-chain trust. Least privilege of the deploy channel: the server should only run code it can prove the owner authored.
findings.recommendation: Require signed commits: after fetch, verify the tip with `git verify-commit origin/$BRANCH` (GPG or SSH-signed via `gpg.format=ssh` + allowed_signers) and abort the ff-merge if verification fails. Alternatively pin to signed tags and deploy only tags matching `v*`. Keep the pull model (it is correct for the outbound-only topology) but gate the merge on a signature check. Also add `git config --local receive.denyNonFastForwards` is N/A here; the ff-only guard already blocks history rewrite locally.

findings.title: Auto-deploy pull ships the hardcoded-secret / RCE scripts onto the running server
findings.evidence: The pull deploy checks out the whole repo (`git checkout -f -b main origin/main`). git ls-files confirms deploy_bridge.py, deploy_tunnel.py, fix_sudo.py, push_templates.py, remote_backfill.py, security_harden.py, server_maintenance.py, telegram_bridge.py are all tracked and thus land in /home/nishan/portfolio. These embed `PASSWORD = "6WKW5_3w2w5121"` and the live Telegram token; the bot script provides shell/Python RCE.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Least privilege of deployed surface + 12-Factor Factor V (release contains only what runtime needs). ASVS V14.2.1 (remove unneeded features/files from production) and V10 (no dangerous code paths shipped).
findings.recommendation: Split the repo: keep runtime code (app.py, models.py, templates, static, fcc_sync.py, requirements.txt, deploy/*.service, auto_deploy.sh) and move all paramiko/SSH one-off ops scripts and telegram_bridge.py out of the deployed tree (separate `ops/` repo not checked out on the server, or a `.deployignore`/sparse-checkout that excludes them). At minimum, purge the hardcoded secrets from history (git filter-repo) before any production cutover, since the pull deploy makes the public repo the literal server contents.

findings.title: /admin content-management route remains unauthenticated
findings.evidence: @app.route("/admin", methods=["GET", "POST"]) def admin(): if request.method == "POST": return _handle_admin_post(). _handle_admin_post saves uploaded files (app.py:344-345) and upserts arbitrary Challenge rows with no session/role check.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: OWASP ASVS V4.1.1/V4.1.3 (enforce access control on every privileged function; deny by default). Least privilege: content mutation must require the admin identity, not anonymous.
findings.recommendation: Add an `@admin_required` decorator that checks `session.get("is_admin")` (or a specific `session["user_id"]` matching the owner) and redirects/403s otherwise; apply to both GET and POST of /admin. Because the OTP/OAuth flows are stubs (S-11), gate admin on a server-side secret compare (e.g. an `ADMIN_KEY` env var checked against a login form) rather than the mock auth until real identity is wired. Do this before the tunnel is public.

findings.title: Hardcoded absolute server paths as defaults instead of pure environment config
findings.evidence: PORTFOLIO_DIR="${PORTFOLIO_DIR:-/home/nishan/portfolio}" (auto_deploy.sh:23, setup_auto_deploy.sh:19) and non-overridable `PORTFOLIO_DIR="/home/nishan/portfolio"` in setup_server.sh:17. Component catalog notes numerous other files also hardcode this path.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: 12-Factor Factor III (config in env, not baked into code) and Factor X (parity via config, not per-host code edits). Least surprise for a production cutover to a different host/user.
findings.recommendation: auto_deploy.sh and setup_auto_deploy.sh already allow override via env (good). Fix setup_server.sh:17 to `PORTFOLIO_DIR="${PORTFOLIO_DIR:-/home/nishan/portfolio}"` and source a single `deploy/deploy.env` (PORTFOLIO_DIR, DEPLOY_BRANCH, PORTFOLIO_SERVICE, REPO_URL) from all three scripts so one file drives every host assumption.

findings.title: Deploy log written under data/ and pip install failures are swallowed
findings.evidence: LOG_FILE="$PORTFOLIO_DIR/data/deploy.log"; pip install line ends with `|| log "WARNING: pip install reported an issue (continuing)."` (continues even if a required new dep failed to install, so the restarted app can crash-loop). nohup fallback writes to /tmp/portfolio.log, separate from data/deploy.log, splitting the audit trail.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: OWASP ASVS V7.1/V7.3 (consistent, complete security-relevant logging) and 12-Factor Factor XI (logs as event streams). A deploy that half-applies (new code, old/failed deps) violates build/release/run atomicity (Factor V).
findings.recommendation: Make pip failure abort the deploy before restart: `|| { log "ERROR: pip install failed; aborting restart"; exit 1; }` so the app keeps running the last-good release. Consider capturing the pre-deploy HEAD to enable an automatic `git reset --hard <prev>` rollback if the post-restart health check fails. Unify logging to data/deploy.log (drop the /tmp fallback along with the nohup path per SU-2).

findings.title: Deploy timer runs as User=nishan (bot's user) — no privilege separation for the deploy actor
findings.evidence: portfolio-deploy.service runs `User=$USER_NAME` (nishan). The same user runs the Telegram bot (RCE surface, 08-security S-3) and owns the checkout. A bot compromise can therefore edit auto_deploy.sh in place or the checkout, which the timer then executes with the scoped restart-sudo.
findings.methodologyPrinciple: Principle of Least Privilege / separation of duties (ASVS V1.4). The deploy actor should not be the same identity as the internet-facing RCE-capable process.
findings.recommendation: For production cutover, run the deploy timer as a dedicated unprivileged `deploy` user that owns the git checkout read-only to `nishan`, or make the checkout owned by root and the timer run as root with the git operations only. At minimum, once the bot is removed/sandboxed (S-3) this collapses; document the dependency. Set the checkout files non-writable by the runtime user so a web/bot compromise cannot alter what the timer runs.

topPriorities: SU-2 + SU-1: Before any public cutover, kill debug mode and the dev server. Author/commit deploy/portfolio.service running gunicorn (Restart=always), guard app.run with FLASK_DEBUG defaulting False, remove auto_deploy.sh's `nohup python app.py` fallback, and make SECRET_KEY fail-closed with a server-side .env (ship .env.example).
SU-6: Add @admin_required (server-side ADMIN_KEY compare, since OTP/OAuth are stubs) gating both GET and POST of /admin before the Cloudflare tunnel is exposed.

SU-3: Delete /etc/sudoers.d/90-nishan-overrides (retire fix_sudo.py & server_maintenance.py sudoers writes) so the scoped 91-portfolio-deploy restart-only rule is actually the effective privilege; validate with visudo -c and sudo -l.

SU-5 + SU-4: Remove the hardcoded-secret/RCE ops scripts from the deployed tree (sparse-checkout/separate ops repo) and purge secrets from git history; gate auto_deploy's ff-merge on `git verify-commit`/signed-tag so the server only runs owner-authored, integrity-verified code.
audits.summary: The new pull-based auto-update design is fundamentally sound for this topology: auto_deploy.sh correctly fetches origin/main without touching the tree, exits quietly when current, ABORTS on any uncommitted local changes (protecting server-only data/.env), does fast-forward-only merges (never rewriting history), and installs deps before restart. The outbound-only pull model (no webhook, no open ports) is the right fit for the Cloudflare-Tunnel host, and setup_auto_deploy.sh's git checkout deliberately preserves untracked data/, venv/, and .env. The scoped sudoers rule (restart portfolio only) is a genuine least-privilege improvement over blanket NOPASSWD.\n\nHowever the security-update posture has concrete design gaps to close before production cutover, all grounded in 12-Factor / least-privilege / OWASP ASVS: (1) there is NO gunicorn/systemd runtime defined anywhere — the only run path is `app.run(debug=True)`, and auto_deploy's fallback literally relaunches the debug server (Werkzeug console = RCE); (2) SECRET_KEY still falls back to a predictable committed default rather than failing closed, and no .env/.env.example exists; (3) the scoped deploy-sudo is undermined by fix_sudo.py's coexisting NOPASSWD:ALL (last-match-wins in sudoers.d), making the scoping cosmetic; (4) the deploy pull trusts origin/main with no commit-signature verification and ships the entire public repo — including the files that embed the live SSH password and Telegram token and the RCE bot — onto the running host; (5) /admin remains fully unauthenticated. Recommended cutover order: harden the runtime (gunicorn + debug off + fail-closed secret) -> auth-gate /admin -> collapse sudo to the single scoped rule -> integrity-verify + slim the deploy payload -> separate the deploy actor from the bot user. Findings SU-3/SU-4/SU-5/SU-9 are server-only/deferred (server unreachable, secret rotation deferred to production); SU-1/SU-2/SU-6/SU-7/SU-8 are code-level and can be implemented now.

plan.foundationFirst: The dependency root is the single design-token layer in static/css/style.css (brand-tokens audit, BT-1/2/3/4/5/6/7). Almost every downstream UI, responsiveness, and accessibility fix references tokens that do not yet exist (--color-accent-petrol, --font-family-mono, the neutral/surface scale, brand-social tokens) or promotes repeated inline strings into token-driven classes. If responsiveness (clamp() type, fluid modal) or the two contrast fixes (A11Y-06/07) land BEFORE the token scale exists, they will re-introduce raw literals that the token pass must then re-touch, causing serial re-edits of the same lines in style.css, home.html, and challenges.html. So the token :root block must be extended first, then literals replaced, then classes extracted — before any responsiveness/contrast tuning that depends on those same declarations. In parallel, the cheapest and safest code-hygiene deletions (dead stats, dead load_challenges, redundant imports) can lead the whole plan because they touch app.py only and unblock the logic/lineage work without colliding with CSS. The plan therefore opens on app.py-only code hygiene (zero CSS conflict), then lands the CSS token foundation, and only afterward layers UI/a11y/responsiveness on top of the now-existing token layer. All server-only items (schema-reconciling migration, cache/compression headers, image derivatives, gunicorn/systemd runtime, sudoers, secret rotation) are deferred; code that reaches the server via GitHub auto-deploy is safe to push now, but changes requiring the live host or a running DB are queued for the production window.

orderedSlices.rationale: Pure app.py deletions with no CSS/template overlap, so they cannot conflict with any later slice. Removes the fabricated home() stats (mastered=total, in_progress=1, always-100% progress that no template renders), the dead load_challenges() filesystem loader (which is also the origin of the phantom mastered/not_started vocabulary and a competing lineage), and redundant function-local imports of markdown/requests/re. Doing this first also resolves lineage finding SSOT-4 (fabricated stats) and SSOT-6 (dead duplicate lineage) at the same edit sites, so logic and lineage stay in sync. Trivial, safe, and shrinks the surface every later reader must reason about.

orderedSlices.slice: DRY consolidation of markdown rendering, FCC-quote fetch, and business rules into helpers/model properties
orderedSlices.rationale: Collapses the markdown.markdown(field or '') duplication across home()/challenge_detail()/api_challenge() (three inconsistent null-guard spellings) into Challenge model properties, and merges get_fcc_quote()/get_daily_quote() onto one _fetch_random_fcc_quote() helper. Centralizing the fcc_description-vs-problem_text precedence into one display property also fixes the lineage inconsistency where the pages disagree. Must follow slice 1 (same file, avoid churn) and must precede any server-only markdown-precompute (PERF-10) so the precompute has a single canonical rendering function to call. app.py-only; reaches server via auto-deploy.

orderedSlices.slice: Extend the :root design-token layer (colors, mono font, neutral/surface scale, brand-social) and remove the dead Inter font
orderedSlices.rationale: THE FOUNDATION. Adds every token the downstream UI/a11y/responsiveness slices need before they run: --color-accent-petrol (#005863), --color-primary-blue-dark (#005a7d hover), --font-family-mono (JetBrains Mono), the named neutral/surface scale (#f8f9fa/#f1f3f5/#e9ecef/#343a40/#212529/#adb5bd + admin grays), --color-border-subtle, and brand-social (--color-brand-github/-hover). Also deletes the never-referenced Inter Google Font. This is additive to style.css only (no literals replaced yet), so it cannot regress any page and creates zero cross-file conflict; it simply makes the vars available. Everything after this consumes these names.
orderedSlices.methodology: W3C Design Tokens + Atomic Design + DRY

orderedSlices.slice: Replace hardcoded hex/font literals across templates with the new tokens (find-and-replace)
orderedSlices.rationale: With the tokens defined (slice 3), swap the literals: petrol #005863 (5 sites), the byte-identical #a70e13 -> var(--color-primary-red) in challenges.html:395, the mono stacks (3 sites), the neutral/surface grays, GitHub #24292e/#1a1e22, and the accent literals (#d63384/#0dcaf0/#198754). This is a low-risk mechanical pass but touches home.html, challenges.html, challenge_detail.html, admin.html, register.html, sql.html, base.html — so it MUST run as one coherent slice before any per-page UI/responsiveness edits reopen those same lines. Ordering it here minimizes the number of times each template line is edited.
dependsOn: Extend the :root design-token layer (colors, mono font, neutral/surface scale, brand-social) and remove the dead Inter font

orderedSlices.slice: Promote repeated inline styles and per-page <style> blocks into shared token-driven classes; reconcile conflicting .markdown-content
orderedSlices.rationale: The structural half of the foundation: collapse duplicated inline badge/dark-header/rating/footer strings into classes (.badge-challenge-number, .on-dark-title, .rating-emoji, .footer-fineprint) and extract the per-page <style> blocks (esp. the two conflicting .markdown-content code rules) into style.css so one canonical layer exists. This is deliberately sequenced AFTER the literal swap (slice 4) and BEFORE the a11y control changes (slice 7), because a11y will convert the same rating spans and dark-header elements into buttons/anchors and add ARIA — doing the class extraction first means the a11y pass edits already-classed elements once instead of fighting inline style strings. Larger, moderate visual-regression risk; verify each rendered page after.
dependsOn: Replace hardcoded hex/font literals across templates with the new tokens (find-and-replace)

orderedSlices.slice: Remove cosmetic anti-copy shields (keydown/contextmenu blockers, pointer-events:none, image oncontextmenu)
orderedSlices.rationale: Delete the base.html document.onkeydown F12/Ctrl+Shift+I/J/Ctrl+U interception, the contextmenu block, and the modal image pointer-events:none + oncontextmenu. KG S-12 confirms these are cosmetic-only and buy zero security while breaking keyboard/AT operability (SC 2.1.1/2.1.4/1.1.1). Sequenced before the interactive-control a11y work because it clears the global keydown clobber that would otherwise interfere with the new keydown handlers added for calendar tiles and the modal. Touches base.html script area and challenges.html modal CSS/attr; both were only lightly touched by the token slices, so minimal conflict.
dependsOn: Promote repeated inline styles and per-page <style> blocks into shared token-driven classes; reconcile conflicting .markdown-content

orderedSlices.slice: Make core interactions keyboard/SR operable: calendar tiles, emoji ratings, lightbox dialog, nav/register/validation ARIA, contrast fixes
orderedSlices.rationale: The heart of the a11y work, grouped because it edits the same elements the class-extraction slice just standardized: convert calendar day divs to real anchors/role=button+keydown (A11Y-03), emoji rating spans to <button aria-label> in a role=group (A11Y-04), the lightbox to role=dialog aria-modal with a focusable <button> close + focus trap/return (A11Y-16), complete navbar-toggler/register-social/OTP/validation ARIA (A11Y-05/10/11/12), add skip link + aria-hidden on decorative icons (A11Y-15/09), label CodeMirror + live-region the test console (A11Y-17/18), fix admin paste-zone focus (A11Y-13). Also apply the two confirmed AA contrast fixes now that the neutral token exists: calendar day-number #adb5bd->accessible token (A11Y-06) and Let's Connect text-white-50->text-white on petrol (A11Y-07). Contrast fixes intentionally deferred to here so they consume/adjust the token layer rather than re-adding literals. FCC-HTML heading sanitization (A11Y-08) is server-only and deferred.
